title: How to Use Chopsticks in Japan 窶・and What Never to Do
excerpt: Using chopsticks correctly in Japan is less about technique and more about knowing the four things that are genuinely offensive. The technique is optional. The four things are not.
heroImageAlt: A pair of wooden chopsticks resting on a ceramic chopstick rest beside a bowl of rice
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The four things you must not do
paragraphs: Tatehashi: standing chopsticks upright in a bowl of rice. This is how rice is offered to the dead at funerals. It is considered deeply inauspicious at a dining table. If you need to put your chopsticks down, use the rest (hashioki), or rest them across the bowl edge. Watashi-hashi: resting chopsticks across the top of a bowl as a signal you are done. This is acceptable in practice, but the correct signal is to set them on the hashioki. Sashi-hashi: spearing food with a chopstick as if it were a fork. For things that are difficult to pick up, ask for a fork 窶・it's available at most restaurants. Mayoi-hashi: hovering chopsticks over dishes while deciding what to take. Pick up from the dish you are pointing at.

sections.heading: Passing food chopstick to chopstick
paragraphs: Passing food directly from one person's chopsticks to another's is associated with the funeral ritual of passing cremated bones. If you want to share food, place it on the other person's plate. This rule is known to most Japanese people and explained to children; seeing foreigners do it is understood as unfamiliarity rather than disrespect, but it is worth knowing.

sections.heading: Technique is not the point
paragraphs: Many restaurants that serve foreign visitors have forks available on request. If you cannot use chopsticks comfortably, ask for a fork. Nobody will be offended. Struggling badly with chopsticks for an entire meal while declining a fork is not a gesture of cultural respect 窶・it is inconvenient for you and creates mild concern for the people eating with you. Use the fork. Practice the chopsticks when you have time and aren't hungry.

sections.heading: Picking up communal dishes
paragraphs: At a shared meal 窶・izakaya, shabu-shabu, hot pot 窶・there may be serving chopsticks (toribashi) for transferring food from the central dish to your plate. If there aren't, the convention is to use the reverse end of your own chopsticks (the end you haven't had in your mouth) for serving. This is understood and widely practiced in informal settings.

sections.heading: Disposable chopsticks
paragraphs: Waribashi are the disposable wooden chopsticks served at most casual restaurants and convenience stores. You split them apart from their joined end. Rubbing them together to remove splinters is considered poor form at a proper restaurant 窶・it implies the chopsticks are of low quality, which implies the restaurant is of low quality. At a convenience store eating lunch on a bench, this rule is not in operation. Context applies.

title: The Rules of Gift Giving in Japan: What to Give, How to Give It, When Not To
excerpt: Gift giving in Japan is governed by a set of conventions that are invisible until you get them wrong. Here is what they are.
heroImageAlt: An elegantly wrapped Japanese gift box with a noshi ribbon on a wooden surface
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The presentation matters as much as the gift
paragraphs: In Japan, the wrapping of a gift is not separate from the gift 窶・it is part of it. A well-chosen item presented unwrapped or in a plastic bag conveys carelessness. A modest item in beautiful wrapping conveys consideration. Department stores in Japan wrap purchases without being asked, and the wrapping is typically done with a precision that takes skill. If you are bringing a gift from abroad, find a way to present it wrapped. The intent is not to deceive about value; it is to communicate that you thought about the presentation.

sections.heading: Numbers and colors to avoid
paragraphs: Avoid gifts in sets of four (shi sounds like the word for death) or nine (ku sounds like the word for suffering). Sets of three, five, or seven are considered auspicious. White wrapping paper, used in funeral contexts, and black and white ribbon combinations are better avoided for celebratory gifts. Red is generally acceptable. Gold is particularly positive.

sections.heading: Gifts are not opened in front of the giver
paragraphs: The Japanese convention is to receive a gift graciously, set it aside, and open it privately later. Opening a gift immediately in front of the person who gave it 窶・examining it, exclaiming over it, asking about the price 窶・is not standard. If a Japanese host opens your gift immediately, it is a gesture of warmth toward you specifically. If they set it aside, do not interpret this as indifference. The gift has been received and will be appreciated; the appreciation is simply expressed differently.

sections.heading: Giving with two hands
paragraphs: Gifts, business cards, money envelopes, and any item given as a gesture of significance should be presented with both hands, accompanied by a slight bow. Receiving should be the same. This is not always possible with large items, but the gesture of offering both hands signals respect and intention. Handing something with one hand while doing something else with the other is fine in a casual context and reads as slightly inattentive in a formal one.

sections.heading: What makes a good gift
paragraphs: The best gifts from abroad: regional foods that can't be bought in Japan, quality items associated with your home country, or something that reflects genuine thought about the recipient. Luxury goods as gifts place the recipient in an uncomfortable position (they feel obligated to reciprocate at an equivalent level). Practical consumables 窶・food, drink, quality cosmetics 窶・avoid this dynamic. The rule of thumb used by Japanese people for omiyage applies equally here: regional, edible, individually portioned, appropriately priced.

sections.heading: When not to give a gift
paragraphs: Do not tip in lieu of a gift. Do not give a gift to someone in a customer service role unless you have an established personal relationship with them. Do not give a gift that clearly exceeds the social context 窶・a very expensive present to a casual acquaintance places an obligation on them that is uncomfortable. The gift economy in Japan operates on calibration: the value of the gift reflects the relationship, and mismatching either direction creates awkwardness.

title: Tattoos and Onsen in Japan: What the Policy Actually Is
excerpt: Most onsen ban tattoos. Fewer than you'd think actually enforce it. Here is how to navigate it honestly.
heroImageAlt: Steam rising from an outdoor onsen rotenburo surrounded by autumn trees
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why the ban exists
paragraphs: The tattoo ban at Japanese onsen traces to the historical association between full-body tattoos (irezumi) and organized crime. The ban was introduced not as a cultural statement about tattoos generally but as a practical measure to keep criminal organizations out of communal bathing facilities. The policy has remained for decades largely through institutional inertia, though attitudes have shifted significantly since the Tokyo Olympics announcement in 2013 brought the issue into public debate.

sections.heading: The official policy vs practice
paragraphs: Official policy at most large resort onsen and hotel spa facilities: no tattoos, regardless of size. Enforcement varies significantly. At a small neighborhood sento (public bath) run by a family for 40 years, nobody is checking. At a large facility near a major tourist area, staff may ask at the entrance. The primary concern has always been the communal nature of the bathing 窶・a heavily tattooed person in a visible position in a shared bath creates discomfort for other guests who have the historical association in mind.

sections.heading: Options if you have tattoos
paragraphs: Private baths (kashikiri buro or kashi-kiriburo): many ryokan and onsen hotels offer private family baths bookable by the hour 窶・a full bath for your group only, with no other guests and therefore no policy. This is often the most relaxing option regardless. Tattoo-friendly facilities: they exist and are increasing in number. Websites like Tattoo-Friendly Japan (search for this specifically) maintain updated lists of facilities with explicit tattoo-welcome policies. Covering: some facilities allow tattoos covered by a waterproof bandage or tattoo cover sheet (sold at some facilities), but this is only workable for small tattoos and not universal.

sections.heading: The honest approach
paragraphs: The most straightforward path: contact any onsen facility you intend to visit in advance and ask. Most facilities have dealt with this question before and can give you a direct answer. Arriving at a facility and discovering the policy on entry, then having a confrontation at the desk, is unpleasant for everyone. The honest approach 窶・ask first, respect the answer 窶・is the correct one regardless of your personal opinion about the policy.

sections.heading: Changing attitudes
paragraphs: Japan's tattoo culture is genuinely changing. Younger Japanese people increasingly have tattoos. The Japan Tourism Agency has encouraged onsen facilities to reconsider blanket bans in favor of more nuanced approaches, particularly given international visitor numbers. Some facilities now operate on a no-visible-tattoo basis rather than a full ban. The situation in 2026 is meaningfully different from 2015, and will continue to evolve. The number of tattoo-friendly facilities available is increasing every year.

title: Meishi: The Japanese Business Card Ritual and Why It Matters
excerpt: In Japan, the business card is not a piece of paper. It is a physical representation of the person. Treating it as stationary is the fastest way to start a professional relationship poorly.
heroImageAlt: A Japanese business card (meishi) held with two hands against a wooden meeting table
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What a business card represents
paragraphs: In Japan, the meishi (business card) exchange is a ritual that establishes who you are, your position, and your company 窶・in one gesture, at the start of every professional interaction. Cards are not handed out casually. They are exchanged at the beginning of a meeting with a formality that can feel significant to those not used to it. Understanding why makes the ritual easier: the card is standing in for a formal introduction that would otherwise take several minutes of verbal exchange.

sections.heading: How to present your card
paragraphs: Hold the card with both hands, text facing the recipient so they can read it immediately, and offer a slight bow as you present it. Say your name and company as you do. If you are handing out cards in a group, the most senior person goes first. Do not hand out cards across a table 窶・walk around or hand directly.

sections.heading: How to receive a card
paragraphs: Receive the card with both hands. Take a moment to read it 窶・not a cursory glance, but actually look at it. Acknowledge the person's name and company. If you are sitting down at a meeting, place the card on the table in front of you in order of seniority (most senior person's card nearest you, others in the order they were received). Do not put the card in your pocket or wallet until the meeting is over. The card on the table means the person is still present in the meeting, literally and symbolically.

sections.heading: What not to do
paragraphs: Do not write on a business card in front of the person who gave it to you. Do not fold it. Do not put it in your back pocket and sit on it. Do not place anything on top of it (not a cup, not another card). These actions read as treating the card 窶・and by extension the person 窶・carelessly. After the meeting, file cards appropriately. Japanese professionals use meishi holders specifically for this purpose.

sections.heading: If you don't have cards
paragraphs: Arriving at a Japanese business meeting without cards is not a catastrophe, but it is noticed. A brief explanation 窶・'I apologize, I have run out of cards' 窶・is appropriate. Most Japanese businesspeople will accept this gracefully. If you are planning any professional interactions in Japan, have cards printed. The Japanese side will often have English on the reverse, and printing the same for your own card is appreciated.

title: How to Order and Drink Sake in Japan Without Getting It Wrong
excerpt: Sake is Japan's most misunderstood drink. Most people drink it too warm, in the wrong glass, with the wrong food. This is how to do it correctly.
heroImageAlt: A small ceramic tokkuri flask and ochoko cups of sake on a wooden table at an izakaya
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Sake is not always drunk hot
paragraphs: The most persistent misunderstanding about sake outside Japan is that it is drunk hot. Sake is drunk at a range of temperatures 窶・cold (reishu), room temperature (jouon), slightly warm (nuru-kan), hot (atsu-kan) 窶・and the temperature used depends on the type of sake and personal preference. Premium ginjo and daiginjo sake are almost always drunk cold or at room temperature; heating them diminishes the delicate aromatics that justify their price. Warmer temperatures suit rougher, more robust honjozo or futsushu. At a good sake restaurant, asking which temperature is recommended for a specific bottle is a reasonable question and will get a thoughtful answer.

sections.heading: You pour for others, not yourself
paragraphs: The social convention around sake at a table is that you pour for others and they pour for you. You do not fill your own cup. If someone's cup is empty and you have the carafe, pour for them. If your cup is empty, hold it up slightly toward whoever has the carafe 窶・this is the signal. At a very informal dinner with close friends this rule is relaxed; at any formal or semi-formal context it applies. Pouring for yourself first is read as either self-centered or unaware of the convention.

sections.heading: The receiving gesture
paragraphs: When someone pours sake for you, you do not just hold the cup. You hold it with both hands, or support it from below with one hand while holding it with the other. This is the equivalent of receiving a gift with both hands 窶・a gesture that says you are receiving attentively. When the pour is done, you drink (or set the cup down), and then you pour in return.

sections.heading: What to order
paragraphs: At an izakaya, the standard entry point is a carafe (tokkuri) rather than a bottle. Carafes come in small (1 go, about 180ml) and large (2 go, 360ml) sizes. The small carafe is the right starting order while you are deciding. For a meal at a sake specialist or a good Japanese restaurant, the menu will have sake listed with classifications (junmai, ginjo, daiginjo). Ask for osusume (what do you recommend) and specify whether you prefer lighter or richer, fruity or dry. The staff will know.

sections.heading: Kanpai
paragraphs: The toast is kanpai. Eyes contact, glasses meet lightly (for sake cups, tap gently 窶・they are fragile), drink at the same time. It is customary to wait for kanpai before drinking, even if the sake arrives before everyone is served. Making eye contact during kanpai is not optional 窶・it is the point of the gesture. Looking away while toasting is the universal bad-omen equivalent and works the same in Japan as anywhere else.

title: The Japanese Breakfast: What It Is and Why It Will Change Your Morning
excerpt: A traditional Japanese breakfast is not a quick meal. It is a composed sequence of small things done well, and eating it once will make every hotel breakfast you eat for the rest of your life feel like a concession.
heroImageAlt: A traditional Japanese breakfast set with rice, miso soup, grilled fish, pickles, and tofu
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What a Japanese breakfast is
paragraphs: A traditional Japanese breakfast (washoku asa-gohan) consists of: steamed white rice, miso soup (with tofu, wakame, and green onion), a piece of grilled fish (typically salmon, mackerel, or sanma), a small selection of pickled vegetables (tsukemono), a square of tofu, a small egg dish (tamagoyaki or onsen tamago), and nori (dried seaweed). Everything arrives simultaneously. Nothing is sweet. Coffee is available separately, usually afterward. This is the standard morning meal across ryokan, traditional hotels, and the home kitchens of people who cook breakfast.

sections.heading: The grilled fish
paragraphs: The grilled fish is the anchor. A properly grilled piece of sake (salmon) or saba (mackerel) 窶・salted and grilled slowly over charcoal or in a specialized fish broiler 窶・has a crisp skin, slightly smoky interior, and a flavor that is to a hotel breakfast buffet as a proper espresso is to an office coffee machine. At a ryokan, this fish was probably grilled minutes before it arrived. Eat it immediately.

sections.heading: Miso soup
paragraphs: Miso soup at a proper breakfast is made from dashi (usually kombu and katsuobushi stock) and miso paste, not from a powder. The difference is apparent. The soup arrives hot in a lacquerware bowl with a lid. Lift the lid, place it on the side inverted, drink directly from the bowl, or use the small spoon provided. Pouring the miso soup over your rice is acceptable in informal settings. Mixing the rice with everything into a kind of porridge 窶・ochazuke-style, though that's technically green tea poured over rice 窶・is a personal approach that some people find comforting.

sections.heading: Where to eat one
paragraphs: The best Japanese breakfast is the one at a ryokan, where it arrives in your room or a dedicated dining room and takes 45 minutes to eat properly. The second-best is at a traditional Japanese hotel (a business hotel that has opted to serve Japanese breakfast rather than a buffet). The kissaten morning set 窶・coffee, toast, sometimes a small egg 窶・is a different, simpler thing entirely. The convenience store breakfast is useful and fast and not the same experience.

sections.heading: What it teaches you
paragraphs: Eating a Japanese breakfast once 窶・properly, without hurrying 窶・resets certain assumptions about what mornings can be. The meal is light enough that you feel alert afterward rather than heavy. The flavors are savory and complex at 7am in a way that feels unusual until it feels obvious. The effort that went into it is visible in every dish. For many visitors, the ryokan breakfast is the meal they remember longest from the trip. Arrive at the dining room at the scheduled time, sit down properly, and give it the 45 minutes it is asking for.

title: The Best Time to Visit Japan: An Honest Month-by-Month Guide
excerpt: Spring cherry blossoms and autumn foliage are spectacular. They are also the two most crowded periods of the year. Here is what every month actually offers.
heroImageAlt: Mount Fuji reflected in a lake surrounded by summer wildflowers
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: January窶擢ebruary: winter, crowds at minimum
paragraphs: January is Japan's quietest month for tourism. After New Year's (January 1窶・, busy with domestic travel), the country empties. Hotels are cheaper. Shinkansen seats are available. Famous sites are accessible at human scale. The weather in Tokyo and Kyoto is cold but manageable 窶・4窶・0ﾂｰC, mostly clear. Onsen towns are at their peak: Hakone, Kinosaki, Beppu. February adds Sapporo's Snow Festival (first week), one of Japan's most-attended winter events. Good months for people who want the experience without the crowd premium.

sections.heading: March窶鄭pril: cherry blossoms
paragraphs: Sakura season typically runs late March to mid-April depending on location and year. Tokyo usually peaks in late March; Kyoto about a week later; northern Japan and higher elevations into late April. The flowers last around one to two weeks at any given location. This is the most visited season, the most expensive, and the most beautiful. Book accommodation four to six months in advance. The parks and famous viewpoints are genuinely crowded. The less-visited spots 窶・riverside embankments, school paths, neighborhood parks 窶・offer the same flowers with a fraction of the people.

sections.heading: May: the sweet spot
paragraphs: Late May (after Golden Week, which runs April 29窶溺ay 5) is one of the best times to visit Japan. Spring is still present. Temperatures are comfortable 窶・15窶・2ﾂｰC in most of central Japan. The golden week crowds have gone. Hotels return to normal pricing. The greenery is at its freshest. May 10窶・0 is consistently underrated.

sections.heading: June窶鄭ugust: summer and humidity
paragraphs: June brings the rainy season (tsuyu) to most of Honshu 窶・two to four weeks of grey skies and intermittent rain that is frustrating for outdoor sightseeing. July and August are hot and humid: 30窶・6ﾂｰC in Tokyo and Osaka with 80%+ humidity. Summer festivals (matsuri) are spectacular but the weather is punishing. Hokkaido is the exception 窶・cool, green, and genuinely pleasant in July and August. If you must visit in summer, either go to Hokkaido or plan your days around early mornings and air-conditioned afternoons.

sections.heading: September窶哲ovember: autumn foliage
paragraphs: September brings typhoon season risk (primarily September窶徹ctober) and the beginning of more comfortable temperatures. By October the weather is excellent: clear days, 15窶・5ﾂｰC, minimal rain. Autumn foliage (koyo) peaks in November 窶・Hokkaido in early October, central Honshu through November. The period from late October through mid-November is the second most visited after cherry blossom season, and prices reflect this. Kyoto in November is busy to the point of requiring strategic planning around early mornings and weekdays.

sections.heading: The recommendation
paragraphs: For a first visit with no fixed seasonal preference: late May or mid-October. For a visit specifically structured around a natural event: cherry blossoms in late March窶兎arly April (plan far ahead), autumn leaves in early-mid November. For minimum crowds and maximum value: January or June (accepting the weather trade-offs of each). The worst time to book without planning is Golden Week (late April窶兎arly May) or the first week of August. Not because they're bad 窶・but because accommodation disappears a year in advance and prices reflect demand.

title: Taking a Taxi in Japan: What to Know Before You Get In
excerpt: Japanese taxis are expensive, exceptionally clean, and almost entirely driven by people who know exactly where they are going. The main things to know are not about navigation.
heroImageAlt: A black Toyota Crown Japanese taxi cab waiting at a Tokyo intersection at night
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The door opens automatically
paragraphs: Japanese taxi doors open and close automatically, controlled by the driver. Do not touch the door. Attempting to open or close it manually will work but is unnecessary and slightly startles the driver. This catches almost every first-time visitor. Stand near the door, wait for it to open, get in. When you arrive, the driver will open it for you. Get out, step away, the door closes.

sections.heading: Show the destination in writing
paragraphs: Most taxi drivers in Japan speak limited English. The reliable approach: have your destination written in Japanese characters, either on your phone (from Google Maps, copy the Japanese name) or on a piece of paper from your hotel. Pronunciation of Western place names in Japanese is often different enough from English pronunciation to cause confusion. A screen showing the map with the destination pinned is universally understood. Your hotel's front desk should always be able to provide you with a card showing the hotel address in Japanese 窶・ask for one at check-in.

sections.heading: Fares and metering
paragraphs: Fares are metered. In Tokyo, the base fare is around ﾂ･500窶・50 for the first 1.2 kilometers, then ﾂ･100 per approximately 300窶・00 meters. A 5-kilometer city ride typically runs ﾂ･1,500窶・,500. Late-night surcharges (after 10pm or 11pm depending on company) add 20窶・0%. There is no tip. Pay exactly what the meter says. Credit cards are accepted in most Tokyo taxis now; IC cards (Suica) are accepted in many. Check the payment stickers on the rear side window before you get in if you're unsure.

sections.heading: When to use a taxi
paragraphs: Taxis are worth the cost in specific situations: after the last train, with heavy luggage between hotel and station, when traveling with elderly or young children, in cities with infrequent bus service, or when the destination is not near a train line. In Tokyo during normal hours, taxis are significantly more expensive than the train and not faster 窶・traffic density means trains win on time and price for most inner-city routes. Outside Tokyo, in smaller cities, taxis become more valuable as transit alternatives are thinner.

sections.heading: Hailing and apps
paragraphs: Taxis in Japan are hailed from the street 窶・stand at the curb, extend your arm, and a cab with a lit red indicator (available for hire) will stop. Do not try to hail a cab at a zebra crossing or intersection. Taxi apps: GO (Japan-specific, major cities, English available), Uber (operates in some Japanese cities but uses licensed taxis rather than private drivers), and DiDi. GO is the most widely used and works in Tokyo, Osaka, Kyoto, and most other large cities. Having it installed before you need it at midnight is a better plan than finding it at midnight.

title: Matsumoto: The Castle Town That Gets Everything Right
excerpt: Two and a half hours from Tokyo, a city with one of Japan's three great original castles, a mountain backdrop, an excellent food scene, and a fraction of Kyoto's visitor numbers. There is no explanation for why more people don't come here.
heroImageAlt: Matsumoto Castle reflected in the surrounding moat on a clear autumn morning with the Japanese Alps behind
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The castle
paragraphs: Matsumoto Castle is one of Japan's twelve surviving original castles 窶・meaning it was not destroyed, bombed, or rebuilt in concrete in the postwar era. The main tower (tenshu) dates to 1593. It is black, which is unusual (most Japanese castles are white), and it sits in a moat surrounded by mountains that the castle architects clearly intended as a backdrop. In spring there are cherry trees around the moat. In autumn there are maple trees. In winter there is snow on the Alps. In summer the castle is reflected in the moat in the early morning before the crowds arrive. Every season offers something specific. Entrance is ﾂ･700.

sections.heading: Inside the castle
paragraphs: The interior is original 窶・steep wooden staircases (more ladder than stair in the upper floors), low beams, the smell of old timber, defensive features like gun ports and stone-drop windows. It is small enough to feel like a real building rather than a museum installation, and large enough to spend an hour in. The views from the top floor extend across the city to the Alps. The interior can be crowded at peak times; arrive at 8:30am when it opens and you will often have the upper floors largely to yourself.

sections.heading: The old town
paragraphs: The Nakamachi and Kaichi districts preserve Edo-period kura (storehouses) that have been converted into craft shops, sake breweries, coffee shops, and restaurants. The streets are genuinely old and genuinely in use 窶・not a preserved historical theme zone but a working neighborhood that has been lived in continuously. The Kaichi School building (1876), built in a hybrid Japanese-Western style unique to Meiji-era architecture, is worth the five-minute detour.

sections.heading: Food and sake
paragraphs: Matsumoto is in Nagano Prefecture, which produces excellent sake, buckwheat, and mountain vegetables. The soba in Matsumoto is made from local buckwheat and is a different thing from the Tokyo variety 窶・coarser, darker, more robust. Mameichi, a small soba restaurant near the castle, is consistently recommended. The local sake breweries in Nakamachi offer tastings. Basho, a specialist sake bar near the station, has one of the better regional sake selections in the area.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: From Tokyo (Shinjuku): the Azusa Limited Express to Matsumoto takes 2h30m, costs around ﾂ･6,500 one-way, and is covered by JR Pass. From Nagoya: 2h by Shinano Limited Express. The city is small enough to walk everywhere once you arrive 窶・the castle is 15 minutes from the station on foot. A single full day is enough for the castle, old town, and a proper meal. Two days allows excursions to Kamikochi (30 minutes by bus, one of Japan's most beautiful alpine valleys) or Hotaka (20 minutes by train, a shrine with an unusual collection of religious art).

sections.heading: The Alps in winter
paragraphs: Matsumoto in winter 窶・December through February 窶・is exceptional if you are prepared for cold. The Alps are fully snowcapped. The castle moat reflects a stark black tower against white mountains on clear mornings. Visitor numbers are low. The onsen around the city (Asama, Utsukushigahara, Shirahone) are at their best in cold weather. Shirahone Onsen specifically, 90 minutes by bus from Matsumoto, is a small valley onsen village that has barely changed since the Edo period and is close to empty outside of August.

title: Kyoto in Three Days: What to Actually See and What to Skip
excerpt: Kyoto has over 1,600 temples and shrines. You will not see them all. Here is how to choose the ones that matter 窶・and what makes them worth choosing over the others.
heroImageAlt: The vermilion torii gates of Fushimi Inari at dawn with no crowds visible
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Day one: the east side
paragraphs: Start at Fushimi Inari. The torii gate path that runs up Mt. Inari is the most photographed image in Japan, and the photographs don't exaggerate it. The path continues for four kilometers past the main tourist zone, thinning to almost nothing by the second kilometer. Arrive at 6:30am or earlier and walk past the first major platform 窶・the mountain path above it is genuinely quiet until 9am. In the afternoon: Kinkaku-ji (the Golden Pavilion). Arrive between 2pm and closing. It is always crowded; go anyway, because the building and its reflection in the pond are one of those things that maintains its impact despite the crowds. This is rarer than it sounds.

sections.heading: Day one: evening in Gion
paragraphs: After 5pm, walk through Gion. The most famous streets (Hanamikoji, Shimbashi) are genuinely beautiful in the evening light. In summer (July), the Gion Matsuri preparations fill the streets with yamahoko floats. In autumn, the maple trees along Shimbashi turn. Come to look, not to photograph 窶・or at minimum, look before you photograph. The neighborhood has been fighting the worst effects of mass tourism for several years, and the experience of being there is different when you are present rather than performing.

sections.heading: Day two: the northwest
paragraphs: Arashiyama is the west side's major district, accessible by the Sagano Scenic Railway or the Randen tram. The bamboo grove is a ten-minute walk from the Arashiyama station and genuinely spectacular 窶・and very crowded by 10am, nearly empty at 7am. After the grove: Tenryu-ji, one of Kyoto's great Zen gardens, with a pond framed by borrowed scenery (shakkei) of the mountains behind it. In autumn the maple trees around the pond are what the photographs in every Japan feature show. After Tenryu-ji, walk the Sagano path north to Jojakko-ji 窶・a moss-covered temple on a hillside that appears in almost no itineraries and is about as far from the crowd as you can get while staying in Arashiyama.

sections.heading: Day two: Nishiki Market
paragraphs: Return to central Kyoto in the afternoon for Nishiki Market 窶・a covered arcade called Kyoto's Kitchen, narrow, parallel to Shijo Street, with stalls selling pickled vegetables, grilled mochi, tofu, Kyoto-style dashi components, fresh yuba, and specialty food items. Eat as you walk (this is one of the exceptions to the no-eating-while-walking rule, since the market format makes stationary eating difficult). The market closes early 窶・most stalls done by 6pm.

sections.heading: Day three: what most itineraries miss
paragraphs: Philosopher's Path (Tetsugaku no Michi): a two-kilometer canal-side walkway between Ginkaku-ji and Nanzen-ji in the northeastern hills. In cherry blossom season the canal is lined with sakura. At any time of year it is quieter than the central tourist circuit and lined with small cafﾃｩs, tofu restaurants, and craft shops. Walk it slowly, south to north. At the northern end: Nanzen-ji, a Zen temple complex with an aqueduct running through the grounds 窶・one of the odder visual combinations in Kyoto and one of the most memorable.

sections.heading: What to skip
paragraphs: Nijo Castle: historically interesting, architecturally significant (the nightingale floor squeaks deliberately to detect intruders), and fairly dry as a visitor experience compared to everything else Kyoto offers. Come if you have four or more days. Toei Kyoto Studio Park (a movie set theme park): enthusiastically recommended by some and entirely optional for everyone who isn't specifically interested in period samurai dramas. Philosopher's Path during peak cherry blossom season: beautiful, but the crowd density removes the contemplative quality that is the entire point.

title: The Unwritten Rules of Japanese Trains 遯ｶ繝ｻRead This Before You Board
excerpt: Japan遯ｶ蜀ｱ trains run on time to the minute and operate on a set of social rules that nobody posts on a sign. Here遯ｶ蜀ｱ what they are.
heroImageAlt: The interior of a Tokyo commuter train with passengers sitting quietly
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Silent mode means silent
paragraphs: The first thing Japanese train operators ask is that you set your phone to manner mode (silent). This means no ringtone, no notification sounds, no speaker audio of any kind. It does not mean vibrate. In a quiet carriage, a buzzing phone on a hard surface is clearly audible to the six people nearest you. Put it fully silent. This takes two seconds and prevents one of the more uncomfortable social experiences available to a visitor.

sections.heading: Phone calls are not done
paragraphs: Taking a phone call on a Japanese commuter train is a social violation roughly equivalent to eating a sandwich in a library. The standard approach is to let it go to voicemail, or to answer, say quietly that you are on a train and will call back, and end the call immediately. This is what Japanese commuters do. On long-distance Shinkansen, calls are generally acceptable in the space between carriages. In the carriage itself, the same rules apply.

sections.heading: Priority seats
paragraphs: The seats marked with a priority label near the carriage doors (often in a different color or fabric) are reserved for elderly passengers, pregnant women, passengers with young children, and those with injuries or disabilities. On a crowded train, these seats fill up like any other. On a less crowded train, the norm is to leave them empty unless you are one of the intended users. The nuance: at 11pm on a Friday, nobody enforces this strictly. At 8am on a Monday, a healthy young person sitting in a priority seat while an elderly passenger stands nearby is making a choice that other passengers will notice.

sections.heading: Platform queuing
paragraphs: Most Tokyo platforms have painted lines on the ground showing where train doors will open and which direction passengers should queue. You queue behind the lines, in two parallel columns, and you let people off the train before you board. This is not complicated, but it is enforced by social pressure rather than barriers, and it fails immediately when visitors don遯ｶ蜀ｲ understand the system. Look at the floor. Find the lines. Stand in them.

sections.heading: Bags and backpacks
paragraphs: On a crowded train, a backpack worn on your back occupies the space of an additional person and hits other passengers every time you turn. The protocol is to take it off and either hold it in front of you or place it in the overhead luggage shelf. Large rolling suitcases go in the space at the end of the carriage near the doors, not in the aisle. This is an issue specifically at peak hours on the Narita Express and on Shinkansen platforms during tourist seasons.

sections.heading: Eating on trains
paragraphs: Eating on local commuter trains is generally not done. Drinking from a closed container is fine. On long-distance Shinkansen, eating is acceptable and expected 遯ｶ繝ｻthe ekiben (station bento) culture exists specifically for this. The distinction is between a 10-minute metro ride and a two-hour bullet train journey. If in doubt, the rule is: if the person next to you isn遯ｶ蜀ｲ eating, you probably shouldn遯ｶ蜀ｲ be either.

sections.heading: Why these rules matter
paragraphs: Japan遯ｶ蜀ｱ train system carries millions of people every day with near-zero conflict. The reason is not enforcement 遯ｶ繝ｻthere are no transit police watching for phone calls or checking priority seats. The reason is that everybody in the carriage has implicitly agreed to the same set of rules, and the rules work because they are followed consistently. A visitor who doesn遯ｶ蜀ｲ know them isn遯ｶ蜀ｲ being judged harshly; they遯ｶ蜀ｳe just outside the system. Knowing them before you board means you spend the journey looking at the city, not at an awkward situation.

title: How to Visit a Japanese Temple or Shrine Without Being Accidentally Rude
excerpt: Temples and shrines have different rules, different purposes, and different gods. Getting them confused is the starting mistake. Everything else follows from there.
heroImageAlt: A stone torii gate leading to a Shinto shrine path lined with lanterns
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Temple vs shrine 遯ｶ繝ｻthe difference matters
paragraphs: Temples (o-tera) are Buddhist. Shrines (jinja) are Shinto. The buildings look different, the rituals are different, and the correct behavior is slightly different at each. The fastest way to tell them apart: if there is a torii gate 遯ｶ繝ｻthe distinctive arch, usually red or stone 遯ｶ繝ｻyou are at a shrine. If there is a large incense burner and a main hall with Buddha imagery, you are at a temple. Many famous sites have both on the same grounds, particularly in Kyoto, because Buddhism and Shinto coexisted for centuries.

sections.heading: The torii gate
paragraphs: Walking through a torii gate marks the transition from the ordinary world into sacred space. Walk through it, not around it. The center of the path through a shrine is considered the route of the deity; some people walk slightly to one side as a gesture of deference. This is not required of visitors, but it遯ｶ蜀ｱ the kind of thing worth knowing exists.

sections.heading: Temizuya: the purification fountain
paragraphs: Near the entrance of both shrines and many temples there is a temizuya 遯ｶ繝ｻa stone basin with ladles. The ritual: take a ladle with your right hand, pour water over your left hand. Transfer the ladle to your left hand, pour over your right. Pour a small amount into your left palm and rinse your mouth (spit to the side, not into the basin). Pour the remaining water from the ladle over both hands simultaneously, tilting the ladle so the water runs down the handle rather than being poured from the bowl. Most foreign visitors skip this entirely and it遯ｶ蜀ｱ not obligatory, but doing it correctly is a straightforward gesture of respect.

sections.heading: How to pray at a shrine
paragraphs: Approach the main hall (haiden). Toss a coin into the offering box 遯ｶ繝ｻfive yen (go-en) coins are traditional because 遯ｶ譫o-en遯ｶ繝ｻalso means 遯ｶ譫ood fortune.遯ｶ繝ｻRing the bell if there is one. Bow deeply twice. Clap twice. Pray or hold the moment. Bow once more. That遯ｶ蜀ｱ the full sequence. You don遯ｶ蜀ｲ need to remember all of it. Two deep bows and two claps gets you 80% of the way there and nobody will correct you.

sections.heading: How to pray at a temple
paragraphs: Buddhist temples don遯ｶ蜀ｲ involve clapping. The general approach: place a coin in the offering box, place your hands together (gassho), bow, and hold the moment. Incense burners at temples allow you to light a stick and wave the smoke toward yourself 遯ｶ繝ｻconsidered purifying, and particularly common for waving smoke toward aching joints. Buy the incense at the counter, not from someone else遯ｶ蜀ｱ bundle.

sections.heading: Photography
paragraphs: Photography of the exterior grounds is almost always permitted. Photography inside the main hall is often not, and there will usually be a sign. If there is no sign and you are unsure, look at what other visitors are doing. Taking a photograph while someone else is praying is poor form regardless of whether signs permit it. At some Kyoto shrines, photography at certain hours has been restricted specifically because of past overcrowding by tourists 遯ｶ繝ｻcheck in advance for Fushimi Inari and Gion Shrine.

sections.heading: What to buy
paragraphs: Omamori are amulets sold at most shrines and temples, intended for specific purposes: safe travel, good health, exam success. They are meant to be replaced annually 遯ｶ繝ｻreturn the old one to the shrine when you buy a new one, or dispose of it respectfully. Ema are wooden plaques for writing wishes and prayers, hung on a rack at the shrine. You don遯ｶ蜀ｲ need to write in Japanese; English works. The wish is between you and whatever you are choosing to address.

title: Why You Should Never Tip in Japan 遯ｶ繝ｻand What to Do Instead
excerpt: In Japan, the service is built into the price. Adding a tip doesn遯ｶ蜀ｲ reward the server 遯ｶ繝ｻit creates an awkward situation for both of you.
heroImageAlt: A server at a traditional Japanese restaurant carefully placing dishes on a low table
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The rule
paragraphs: Do not tip in Japan. Not at restaurants. Not in taxis. Not at the ryokan. Not at the hotel. The rule applies everywhere, with essentially no exceptions. This is not a matter of the tip being too small or the service not meriting it. Tipping is simply not part of the service culture, and attempting it creates confusion rather than goodwill.

sections.heading: Why tipping is not done
paragraphs: In Japan, service is understood as part of what you paid for. The waiter who refills your water, the taxi driver who opens and closes the door automatically from the front seat, the ryokan attendant who arranges your futon 遯ｶ繝ｻthey are doing their job with the full understanding that it will be done to a high standard because that is the expectation, not because there is a financial incentive to exceed it. Tipping implies that the service was beyond what was expected, which suggests the expectation was low. This is not the message most people intend to send.

sections.heading: What happens if you try
paragraphs: If you leave cash on the table at a restaurant, staff will often chase you down the street to return it, assuming you forgot it. If you hand money directly to a server as a tip, it is likely to be declined, or accepted with visible discomfort. In rare cases it may be accepted with genuine bewilderment. None of these outcomes is the one you were hoping for.

sections.heading: At a ryokan
paragraphs: Ryokan are the one context where something resembling a tip exists 遯ｶ繝ｻbut it operates on different logic. An o-shibori (a small envelope with a gratuity inside) can be given to the ryokan attendant assigned to your room on arrival, as a gesture of appreciation for the personal service throughout the stay. This is not expected and is not common among foreign guests. If you want to do it, the amount is typically ・ゑｽ･1,000遯ｶ繝ｻ,000, placed in an envelope (not loose cash, never loose cash). The envelope matters as much as the money.

sections.heading: In taxis
paragraphs: Taxi fare is metered. You pay the meter. You do not round up. You do not add anything. The driver will make precise change for whatever you give them, and expect you to take it. Declining the change and saying 遯ｶ魏親ep it遯ｶ繝ｻwill land as strange, not generous.

sections.heading: What to do instead
paragraphs: What Japanese culture does value in place of tips: acknowledgment. A genuine 遯ｶ蜥罫igatou gozaimashita遯ｶ繝ｻ(thank you very much) at the end of a meal, a bow at the door of the ryokan, a simple gesture of appreciation that the person can receive without awkwardness. The service culture in Japan is built on mutual respect that doesn遯ｶ蜀ｲ require cash to function. The best thing you can do is participate in it on its own terms.

title: When to Take Your Shoes Off in Japan: The Complete Guide
excerpt: The shoe rule in Japan is not arbitrary. It reflects a clear line between outside space and inside space that structures daily life. Here is where that line is drawn.
heroImageAlt: A row of shoes neatly arranged in a traditional Japanese entryway genkan
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The genkan
paragraphs: Traditional Japanese homes, ryokan, and many older buildings have a genkan 遯ｶ繝ｻan entrance area slightly lower than the rest of the floor, designed specifically for removing shoes. You step into the genkan, take off your shoes, and step up into the interior in socks or bare feet. The directional convention: shoes are placed pointing toward the door, so they遯ｶ蜀ｳe ready to put on when you leave. This isn遯ｶ蜀ｲ required of guests, but it遯ｶ蜀ｱ what you遯ｶ蜀､l observe.

sections.heading: At ryokan
paragraphs: At a ryokan, shoes come off at the main entrance and you遯ｶ蜀ｳe given slippers. These slippers go everywhere inside the building except tatami rooms (where you go in socks or bare feet) and the toilet (which has its own dedicated pair). The slippers-off-at-tatami rule catches most visitors at least once. Look at the floor: if it遯ｶ蜀ｱ tatami, your slippers stay outside.

sections.heading: At some restaurants
paragraphs: Traditional Japanese restaurants 遯ｶ繝ｻparticularly those with tatami seating or private rooms 遯ｶ繝ｻwill have a designated shoe removal area near the entrance. The signal is usually a raised floor, a row of slippers, or a staff member making the gesture. If you遯ｶ蜀ｳe unsure, look at what other guests near the entrance are doing. Western-style restaurants in Japan do not require shoe removal.

sections.heading: At some temples
paragraphs: Certain temple halls require shoes to be removed before entering. There will be a step at the entrance and either a rack for shoes or plastic bags to carry them inside with you. This varies by temple. Fushimi Inari in Kyoto does not require shoe removal for the main torii paths. Todai-ji in Nara does for the main hall interior. The entrance step is the consistent signal.

sections.heading: The toilet slipper trap
paragraphs: The most reliable mistake visitors make: walking out of the toilet in the toilet slippers. At ryokan and some traditional homes, there is a dedicated pair of slippers inside the toilet room. You put them on to enter, and you take them off when you leave. Walking back through the inn in toilet slippers is the equivalent of walking through the house carrying the toilet brush. It happens to almost everyone once. The correction is to look down at your feet before exiting the toilet.

sections.heading: Reading the signals
paragraphs: The consistent signal that shoes come off: a step at the entrance that marks a change in floor level. When you see a step, assume shoes off until you have evidence otherwise. Modern hotels, convenience stores, restaurants with Western seating, and most commercial buildings do not follow this rule. The genkan-step is the dividing line.

title: How to Behave in Public in Japan: The Unwritten Rules
excerpt: Japanese public spaces operate on a set of unspoken rules that make them exceptionally pleasant. Most visitors don遯ｶ蜀ｲ know them. All of them are learnable in ten minutes.
heroImageAlt: A busy pedestrian crossing in Tokyo with people moving in orderly streams
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Don遯ｶ蜀ｲ eat while walking
paragraphs: Eating while walking is generally not done in Japan. The exception is festival food from stalls at a matsuri (festival), where the eating-while-standing context is explicit. The rule applies elsewhere: you finish your konbini onigiri standing outside the shop, not while walking down the street. Street food vendors at tourist sites have started providing small eating areas specifically because this norm exists and tourists don遯ｶ蜀ｲ know it. Use them.

sections.heading: Queuing is not optional
paragraphs: Japan queues. It queues at ATMs, at crosswalks, at train doors, at ramen shops, at theme park rides. Cutting in line is an extremely unwelcome behavior. If there is no visible queue but a crowd, look for the end of it and join there. Lines at Japanese institutions are remarkably orderly and self-policing 遯ｶ繝ｻno one will say anything if you push in, but everyone will notice, and the social discomfort this creates for you is real and immediate.

sections.heading: Noise levels
paragraphs: Japan is quieter than most countries in public spaces. Not silent 遯ｶ繝ｻShinjuku at 6pm is genuinely loud 遯ｶ繝ｻbut the background noise level in trains, restaurants, and streets comes from the environment rather than individual conversations. Speaking at a volume that carries beyond your table in a restaurant, laughing loudly in a way that commands the room遯ｶ蜀ｱ attention, or holding a speakerphone conversation in a convenience store are all behaviors that will cause people to move away from you. This is the social feedback system.

sections.heading: Escalators
paragraphs: On escalators, the standing side is the left. The right side is for walking. This is the Tokyo convention; Osaka does it in reverse (stand right, walk left) and this causes visible confusion at every point where Tokyoites and Osakans intersect. When in doubt, follow the person in front of you. The escalator rule is enforced entirely by social pressure and the occasional polite request to stand to one side, but it is real and consistent.

sections.heading: There are no rubbish bins
paragraphs: Japan removed most public rubbish bins following a 1995 sarin gas attack. This means that in most public spaces 遯ｶ繝ｻstreets, parks, station platforms 遯ｶ繝ｻthere is nowhere to throw away your waste. You carry it with you until you reach a convenience store (which has a bin near the register), a restaurant, or your accommodation. Konbini is the practical answer for most of the day. This is not as inconvenient as it sounds once you accept that the rubbish is coming with you.

sections.heading: Photography of strangers
paragraphs: Photographing people without consent in Japan operates under the same social norms as most countries, but with one additional consideration: the geisha districts of Kyoto (particularly Gion) have become sites of harassment as visitors photograph geiko and maiko at close range. Some streets in Gion now restrict photography entirely during certain hours. Photographing someone in traditional dress without asking, following them for a shot, or blocking a path for a photograph are all behaviors that have become serious enough to prompt legal responses in some areas. In all other contexts: use judgment, ask when in doubt, and take no for an answer.

title: Japan Still Runs on Cash 遯ｶ繝ｻHere遯ｶ蜀ｱ How Much to Bring and Where to Get It
excerpt: Cards are increasingly accepted in Japan, but 遯ｶ莠冢creasingly遯ｶ繝ｻis not the same as 遯ｶ閻鋲liably.遯ｶ繝ｻPlan for cash and treat the card acceptance as a bonus.
heroImageAlt: A hand holding Japanese yen banknotes in front of a temple market
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why Japan still uses cash
paragraphs: Japan遯ｶ蜀ｱ cash culture is not technological backwardness. It reflects a combination of historical distrust of credit, very low crime rates that make carrying cash safe, a retail infrastructure built around cash transactions, and a cultural preference for precise payments. The ・ゑｽ･100, ・ゑｽ･500, and ・ゑｽ･1,000 denominations handle most daily transactions efficiently. Japan also has one of the lowest cash crime rates in the world 遯ｶ繝ｻlosing a wallet in Tokyo has a high probability of recovery.

sections.heading: Is it changing
paragraphs: Yes, slowly. Since 2020, card acceptance has expanded significantly 遯ｶ繝ｻmajor chains (convenience stores, department stores, chain restaurants) almost universally accept Visa and Mastercard now. But neighborhood restaurants, small temple admission counters, some bus routes, onsen entrance fees, market stalls, and many rural businesses remain cash-only. You will encounter cash-only situations, and the choice of whether to have cash available is not really a choice.

sections.heading: How much to bring
paragraphs: A reasonable daily cash budget for someone eating well and visiting paid attractions is ・ゑｽ･5,000遯ｶ繝ｻ,000 per day. For a one-week trip to Tokyo and Kyoto, ・ゑｽ･50,000遯ｶ繝ｻ0,000 in cash is a workable target, supplemented by card use where available. Carry ・ゑｽ･10,000遯ｶ繝ｻ0,000 on you at any given time 遯ｶ繝ｻenough for a day遯ｶ蜀ｱ expenses with a buffer. More than ・ゑｽ･30,000 in a wallet is unnecessary given the ATM density in Japan.

sections.heading: The 7-Eleven ATM
paragraphs: The most reliable cash source for foreign visitors: 7-Bank ATMs at 7-Eleven convenience stores. These accept almost all international cards, have English menus, charge a flat fee (around ・ゑｽ･110遯ｶ繝ｻ20 depending on your bank), and are open 24 hours. Japan Post ATMs (in post offices) and some AEON bank ATMs are also reliable. Traditional Japanese bank ATMs 遯ｶ繝ｻMizuho, MUFG, SMBC 遯ｶ繝ｻfrequently do not accept foreign cards or only accept them during banking hours. Use the convenience store ATMs.

sections.heading: Where cards are now accepted
paragraphs: Cards work reliably at: hotel check-in and checkout, department stores and major shopping centers, chain restaurants (Yoshinoya, Sukiya, many ramen chains), most supermarkets, Shinkansen ticket machines (for online booking pickup), pharmacy chains, and tourist attractions in major cities. Cards work unreliably or not at all at: local izakaya, neighborhood restaurants, temples and shrines, market stalls, some local buses, small guesthouses, and anything described as 遯ｶ諠疎mily run.遯ｶ繝ｻWhen you see a menu without a payment logo visible, assume cash.

sections.heading: The IC card exception
paragraphs: The IC card (Suica or ICOCA) sits between cash and card and is worth treating separately. It functions as a prepaid card accepted on trains, buses, and at convenience stores, and it can be loaded at any JR ticket machine with cash. Load it from a 7-Eleven ATM withdrawal, use it everywhere it遯ｶ蜀ｱ accepted, and keep ・ゑｽ･2,000+ on it at all times. The combination of an IC card and a cash reserve handles 95% of daily transactions without any friction.

title: SIM Card vs Pocket Wi-Fi in Japan: What Nobody Tells You Before You Choose
excerpt: Both work. One is significantly more convenient. The answer has changed in the last three years and most travel articles haven遯ｶ蜀ｲ caught up.
heroImageAlt: A person holding a phone with a Japan map open in a busy train station
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why you need internet
paragraphs: Navigation in Japan without internet access is not impossible 遯ｶ繝ｻthe signage in major train stations is multilingual and the numbered exit system at Tokyo Metro stations is excellent 遯ｶ繝ｻbut it is significantly harder. Google Maps with Japanese public transit data is the most useful single tool available to a visitor, and it requires a live connection to function well. Sort this before you arrive.

sections.heading: Pocket Wi-Fi: the case for
paragraphs: A pocket Wi-Fi is a small device you carry that creates a personal Wi-Fi hotspot using a Japanese mobile network. The main advantages: it can connect multiple devices simultaneously (useful if you遯ｶ蜀ｳe traveling with a partner or need a tablet for navigation), it doesn遯ｶ蜀ｲ require changing your phone遯ｶ蜀ｱ SIM or settings, and it遯ｶ蜀ｱ been the standard solution for visitors for years 遯ｶ繝ｻairport rental desks are efficient and the return process is a prepaid envelope drop.

sections.heading: Pocket Wi-Fi: the case against
paragraphs: It遯ｶ蜀ｱ one more thing to carry and charge. The battery typically lasts 8遯ｶ繝ｻ0 hours under normal use, which is not a full travel day. You need to remember it. If you leave it at the hotel, you have no internet until you get back. The data may be throttled after 3遯ｶ繝ｻGB per day depending on the provider, which matters if you遯ｶ蜀ｳe using video navigation or uploading photos.

sections.heading: SIM cards
paragraphs: A Japanese tourist SIM replaces your existing SIM and gives you a Japanese data connection. Data-only SIMs (no calls, no texts) run about ・ゑｽ･3,000遯ｶ繝ｻ,000 for 15遯ｶ繝ｻ0 days with 5遯ｶ繝ｻ0GB. Voice SIMs are more expensive. The advantages: you carry nothing extra, battery life is your phone遯ｶ蜀ｱ own, and you遯ｶ蜀ｳe always connected. The disadvantages: you need to physically swap SIMs, store your original one safely, and configure the APN settings (which take 5 minutes and require looking up your provider).

sections.heading: eSIM: the best option now
paragraphs: If your phone supports eSIM (most phones released after 2020 do), a Japanese eSIM is the cleanest solution. You download the profile before you arrive, activate it at the airport, and your phone connects to a Japanese network while keeping your original SIM active for calls and texts from home. No physical swap, no extra device, no configuration beyond installing the profile. Providers like IIJmio, Ubigi, Airalo, and several others offer Japan eSIMs starting around $15遯ｶ繝ｻ5 USD for 7遯ｶ繝ｻ0 days. Check compatibility before you buy.

sections.heading: Where to get them
paragraphs: Pocket Wi-Fi rental counters are at Narita, Haneda, Kansai, and most other major airports. Booking in advance online is cheaper than walk-up rental. Physical SIMs are sold at airport convenience stores and electronics retailers (Bic Camera, Yodobashi). eSIMs are purchased online before departure 遯ｶ繝ｻactivate them when you land. For a trip of 5遯ｶ繝ｻ4 days with a single traveler, the eSIM or a basic tourist SIM is the practical recommendation.

title: The Only Japanese Phrases You Actually Need
excerpt: Japanese is difficult. A handful of phrases isn遯ｶ蜀ｲ. The ten expressions below will handle the majority of situations a visitor encounters, and they take about 20 minutes to learn.
heroImageAlt: A Japanese menu board with handwritten chalk specials at a small restaurant
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why bother
paragraphs: English in Japan is available, patchily. Major chain restaurants have English menus or picture menus. Train stations in Tokyo and Kyoto are multilingual. Hotel staff at international properties speak English. Beyond these contexts 遯ｶ繝ｻat the neighborhood izakaya, the market stall, the onsen town遯ｶ蜀ｱ local restaurant 遯ｶ繝ｻJapanese is what you have. A few phrases, used with reasonable pronunciation and a willingness to repeat them, will get you through almost everything. Nobody expects fluency. They do notice the attempt.

sections.heading: The four daily essentials
paragraphs: Sumimasen 遯ｶ繝ｻexcuse me, sorry to bother you. The Swiss Army knife of Japanese phrases. Use it to get attention in a restaurant, to apologize for bumping into someone, to start a question, to ask for help. Arigatou gozaimasu 遯ｶ繝ｻthank you very much. The formal version. Useful for anything beyond casual thanks. Hai 遯ｶ繝ｻyes. More affirmative than it translates; also used to acknowledge that you heard something. Ie / iie 遯ｶ繝ｻno. Softer than it sounds. These four phrases, repeated calmly and clearly, handle a remarkable proportion of basic daily interactions.

sections.heading: At restaurants
paragraphs: Kore wo kudasai 遯ｶ繝ｻthis please. Point at the menu item. Three words, works everywhere. Hitotsu / futatsu / mittsu 遯ｶ繝ｻone / two / three. Ordering quantities. O-kaikei kudasai 遯ｶ繝ｻthe bill please. You can also do the universal rectangular-finger-gesture (tracing a rectangle in the air with both index fingers) and everyone understands. Oishii 遯ｶ繝ｻdelicious. Using this mid-meal in a small restaurant will reliably cause the chef to emerge from the kitchen.

sections.heading: Getting around
paragraphs: [ Destination ] wa doko desu ka? 遯ｶ繝ｻWhere is [ destination ]? Fill in the location name. Show the name written down and follow this phrase with the map on your phone and you will receive directions, usually very thorough directions, sometimes with an escort. Chotto matte kudasai 遯ｶ繝ｻplease wait a moment. For when someone is talking faster than you can process.

sections.heading: Shopping
paragraphs: Ikura desu ka? 遯ｶ繝ｻHow much is it? Most shops will simply type the price on a calculator or show you the price tag, but the question opens the interaction correctly. Kore wa nan desu ka? 遯ｶ繝ｻWhat is this? For menus and market stalls where the item is unidentifiable. Expect a demonstration.

sections.heading: Pronunciation
paragraphs: Japanese vowels are consistent: A is 遯ｶ蜥敬,遯ｶ繝ｻI is 遯ｶ閼㌃,遯ｶ繝ｻU is 遯ｶ讀o,遯ｶ繝ｻE is 遯ｶ閼㍗,遯ｶ繝ｻO is 遯ｶ讀h.遯ｶ繝ｻThey don遯ｶ蜀ｲ shift the way English vowels do. 遯ｶ騾｣rigatou遯ｶ繝ｻis pronounced ah-ree-gah-toh, not ah-rig-uh-toe. Getting the vowels right matters more than anything else and takes five minutes of practice. Japanese people will meet you much more than halfway on pronunciation as long as the vowels are approximately correct.

title: The Tokyo Subway: How to Navigate It Without Getting Lost
excerpt: Tokyo遯ｶ蜀ｱ train network is the most complex urban rail system in the world. It is also, once you understand the basics, extremely easy to use.
heroImageAlt: The platform at Shinjuku Station during morning rush hour with commuters boarding a train
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Two networks, one system
paragraphs: Tokyo遯ｶ蜀ｱ rail network is operated by multiple companies, of which two matter most to visitors: Tokyo Metro (13 lines, run by Tokyo Metro Co.) and Toei Subway (4 lines, run by the Tokyo Metropolitan Government). They connect at major stations, but they are separate systems with separate fares 遯ｶ繝ｻtransferring between them costs extra unless you have an IC card, which handles the fare calculation automatically. On an IC card, the transfer cost is minimal and seamless. On a paper ticket, you遯ｶ蜀､l need to buy a new one each time. Get the IC card.

sections.heading: The Yamanote Line
paragraphs: The Yamanote Line is not a subway 遯ｶ繝ｻit遯ｶ蜀ｱ a JR surface rail loop that runs around central Tokyo, connecting Shinjuku, Shibuya, Harajuku, Ebisu, Osaki, Shinagawa, Tokyo, Akihabara, Ueno, Nippori, Ikebukuro, and back. It takes about an hour to complete the full loop. Think of it as your outer ring: when you want to move between major neighborhoods, this is usually how you do it. Frequency during peak hours is every two to three minutes.

sections.heading: IC card vs paper ticket
paragraphs: Buy a Suica IC card at any JR ticket machine at the airport or a major station. Load it with ・ゑｽ･2,000遯ｶ繝ｻ,000 to start. Tap in, tap out, and the fare is deducted automatically. You never need to calculate fares or buy destination-specific tickets. A paper ticket requires you to select the exact destination and correct fare before you board, which requires reading a map that is entirely in Japanese at some smaller stations. There is no scenario where a paper ticket is preferable to an IC card.

sections.heading: Reading the maps
paragraphs: Tokyo Metro maps are color-coded by line and numbered by station. Each line has a letter prefix and each station a number (e.g., G07 for Ginza on the Ginza line). This number system is displayed on platform signs and inside carriages, which means you can navigate without reading kanji at all. The printed maps at train stations are free and multi-language. Inside the carriages, digital displays above the doors show the next station in Japanese, English, Chinese, and Korean.

sections.heading: Google Maps
paragraphs: Google Maps with transit mode is accurate and reliable for Tokyo public transport. Enter your destination, select the transit option, and it will show you the correct line, platform, number of stops, and transfer instructions. The journey planner also shows fare estimates. Use it as your primary navigation tool. Japan-specific apps like HyperDia and Navitime are more detailed for complex Shinkansen bookings but offer nothing additional for basic subway navigation.

sections.heading: The last train
paragraphs: Tokyo遯ｶ蜀ｱ trains stop running around midnight (times vary by line and station; check Google Maps for the exact last train). This is not like New York or London where the service continues through the night with reduced frequency. The trains stop, full stop. Taxis are available but expensive after midnight and in short supply on Friday and Saturday nights. If you遯ｶ蜀ｳe out late, plan your route in advance and know your last train time. Getting stranded at midnight in Shibuya is solvable but inconvenient and expensive.

title: What You Can and Cannot Photograph in Japan
excerpt: Japan is exceptionally photogenic. It also has specific places and situations where photography is restricted, disrespectful, or now actively prohibited. Knowing the difference matters.
heroImageAlt: A photographer framing a traditional Japanese street scene at dawn with no other people visible
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The general principle
paragraphs: In most outdoor public spaces in Japan, photography is unrestricted. Streets, parks, temple grounds, shrine pathways, market areas, train stations 遯ｶ繝ｻall are freely photographable. The restrictions apply inside specific buildings, in areas where signage prohibits it, and in situations where the photography causes problems for other people or for the place itself. Read the signs, watch what other people are doing, and when in doubt, ask.

sections.heading: Inside temples and shrines
paragraphs: Interior photography inside the main hall of a temple or shrine is usually prohibited. The sign will typically show a camera with a red line through it. Some places allow photography inside with a fee; some famous main halls (Kinkaku-ji遯ｶ蜀ｱ interior, Todai-ji遯ｶ蜀ｱ Buddha) are not accessible at all. Gardens attached to temples are generally freely photographable. Detailed garden photography is expected 遯ｶ繝ｻit遯ｶ蜀ｱ one of the primary reasons people visit.

sections.heading: At onsen and public baths
paragraphs: Photography is prohibited at onsen and sento (public baths), including in the changing rooms. This is absolute and enforced. Phones are not permitted in the bathing areas of most facilities, and taking your phone into a bathing area is both a rule violation and a privacy invasion of every other person present. Leave the phone in the locker.

sections.heading: Photographing strangers
paragraphs: Japan doesn遯ｶ蜀ｲ have a specific law against street photography, but the social norms around photographing strangers are similar to those in most countries: pointing a camera directly at someone遯ｶ蜀ｱ face without consent, following someone for a photograph, or photographing children without parental consent all cross reasonable lines. Most Japanese people will not object verbally, but this reflects a cultural reluctance to confront rather than permission.

sections.heading: In Gion, Kyoto
paragraphs: Gion and the surrounding geisha districts of Kyoto are a specific and documented problem. Years of tourists surrounding and following maiko and geiko for photographs has led to complaints from residents, businesses, and the performers themselves. Some streets now have photography restriction signs during evening hours. Obeying these is not optional 遯ｶ繝ｻlocal authorities have the ability to issue fines. More simply: if you see a maiko walking to an appointment, she is not a photo opportunity. She is a person doing her job.

sections.heading: On trains
paragraphs: Photography on trains is not explicitly prohibited but is not commonly practiced for good reason: photographing other passengers without consent in an enclosed space they cannot leave is uncomfortable regardless of country. Photographing the view from the window is universally fine. Photographing your own group is fine. Pointing a camera at strangers in a carriage is not.

title: Your First Ryokan: What to Expect, What to Do, and What Not to Wear
excerpt: A traditional Japanese inn is not a hotel. The sooner you accept that, the better the stay.
heroImageAlt: A traditional ryokan room with tatami floor, low table, and garden view
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Check-in, checkout, and why timing matters
paragraphs: The check-in time at a ryokan is usually 3pm and checkout is 10am. These aren't suggestions. Your room is being transformed twice a day 窶・futon laid out at turndown, rolled away before breakfast 窶・and that choreography is coordinated with dinner and bath times. Arriving at 6pm and expecting your room to be ready the way a hotel room is always ready will result in confusion on both sides.

sections.heading: Shoes, slippers, and one rule about the toilet
paragraphs: When you arrive, you remove your shoes at the entrance and switch into slippers. The slippers go everywhere except tatami rooms 窶・in tatami rooms you go barefoot or in socks. There will be a separate pair of toilet slippers in the bathroom. Put them on to enter, take them off to leave. Forgetting and wandering the hallway in toilet slippers is the ryokan equivalent of walking through a dinner party in a life jacket.

sections.heading: Your room
paragraphs: Your room will have: a low table, zabuton cushions, a tokonoma (a small alcove, usually with a seasonal arrangement), a yukata folded somewhere, and a kettle with tea. The yukata is your uniform. Wear it to the onsen, to dinner if the place is traditional, back to your room. It's a cotton robe, not a costume. Left side over right 窶・right over left is how the dead are dressed.

sections.heading: The onsen
paragraphs: The onsen, if there is one, operates on a schedule 窶・typically gender-segregated, sometimes with rotating access to different baths. The rules: wash thoroughly at the shower station before entering the communal water. Don't bring a towel into the bath. The small hand towel goes on your head or beside the tub. Don't get in if you've been drinking heavily 窶・people faint, and it's embarrassing for everyone.

sections.heading: Kaiseki dinner
paragraphs: Dinner is kaiseki 窶・a multi-course meal built around seasonal ingredients. It arrives in a sequence you don't choose and can't rush. Don't ask for the menu in advance. Don't request variations unless you have a genuine allergy. The meal takes 90 minutes to two hours, the plates will be small, and you will feel, briefly, like you're not getting enough food. You are getting enough food. The point is rhythm.

sections.heading: Breakfast
paragraphs: Breakfast follows the same logic: rice, miso soup, pickled vegetables, grilled fish, tofu, a small egg dish. It arrives at a fixed time, usually between 7:30 and 9am. It is also the best breakfast in the world, and I say this as someone who is not generally enthusiastic about breakfast.

sections.heading: What it costs
paragraphs: Prices run from around ﾂ･8,000 per person at a decent mid-range option with onsen and breakfast to ﾂ･80,000+ per person at the higher end with full kaiseki. The sweet spot is in the ﾂ･15,000窶督･25,000 range 窶・particularly in onsen towns like Kinosaki Onsen in Hyogo or Gero in Gifu, which don't have the premium of Hakone or Kyoto but are genuinely excellent.

sections.heading: On language
paragraphs: A final note on language. The staff will likely not speak much English, and that's genuinely fine. The experience requires very little language to work. 'Arigatou gozaimasu' (thank you very much) and 'oishii' (delicious) will handle 80% of situations. The rest comes from gestures, patience, and the fact that the people running a ryokan have been doing this for decades and know how to communicate without words.

title: Why January Is the Best Month to Visit Japan (Yes, Really)
excerpt: No cherry blossoms. No 38-degree humidity. No crowds. January is when Japan stops performing for tourists and just exists.
heroImageAlt: Snow-dusted temple grounds in Kyoto in winter
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why even consider January
paragraphs: Summer in Japan is spectacular and exhausting. Cherry blossom season is magical and so crowded that the famous spots feel like theme parks. Autumn foliage is genuinely among the most beautiful things in the natural world and is also, by October, completely overrun. January has none of these problems, because January has a reputation for cold and grey that is only partly deserved.

sections.heading: The actual weather
paragraphs: The actual weather in Tokyo in January: between 4ﾂｰC and 10ﾂｰC during the day, clear skies more often than you'd expect, occasional rain, rare snow. Not harsh. A good wool coat is enough. Kyoto gets occasional snow 窶・which on temple grounds with grey stones and winter moss looks exactly like the photograph you've seen your whole life, except there are almost no other people in it.

sections.heading: Where the crowds went
paragraphs: What January removes: the tourist surge disappears after New Year's (January 1窶・), when domestic travelers return home and the holiday-season international visitors are gone. From January 5th onwards, you can visit Kinkaku-ji without being engulfed in 2,000 people. You can walk the Arashiyama bamboo grove with no one in your frame. This is not an exaggeration. On a cold January Tuesday, the path is genuinely empty.

sections.heading: New Year rituals
paragraphs: What January adds: the rituals of New Year's 窶・hatsumode, the first shrine visit of the year 窶・give the first week of January a feeling of purposefulness that ordinary months don't have. Major shrines are packed January 1窶・ and quieter but still festive through the first week. Asakusa's Senso-ji at 6am on January 2nd is one of the best crowds in the world to be part of.

sections.heading: Onsen towns in winter
paragraphs: Onsen towns are at their best in winter, and this is not a small thing. The specific pleasure of sitting in an outdoor rotenburo 窶・a hot spring bath 窶・with snow falling and steam rising is not available in July. Hakone from Tokyo, Kinosaki Onsen from Kyoto, Beppu in Kyushu: these are all better in winter on nearly every metric. The baths are hotter, the air is colder, and the bowl of food you eat afterwards is twice as satisfying.

sections.heading: Skiing in Hokkaido
paragraphs: Ski resorts: Japan has some of the best powder snow in the world. Hokkaido specifically 窶・Niseko, Furano, Rusutsu 窶・has developed into a serious international ski destination, partly because the light dry snow that falls there is genuinely exceptional. January is peak season. If skiing is relevant to your trip, January in Hokkaido is not a consolation prize. It's the main event.

sections.heading: Flights, hotels, and prices
paragraphs: The practical case: flights cost less in January than in October or April. Hotels cost less. Popular restaurants with months-long waiting lists have more availability. The ryokan in Hakone that has no rooms in November has rooms in January, often at a meaningfully lower rate.

sections.heading: What winter reveals
paragraphs: What Japan offers in winter that it can't offer in spring or autumn: something closer to daily life. When a place is over-visited, it optimizes for visitors. When it's not being watched, it relaxes. January Japan is commuters and schoolchildren and the ramen shop that opens at 6:30am for the people who work early shifts. That Japan is worth seeing, and it's more accessible than people think.

title: The Convenience Store Is Japan's Greatest Culinary Achievement
excerpt: Seven-Eleven in Japan is not Seven-Eleven anywhere else. This distinction matters and deserves your full attention.
heroImageAlt: Onigiri and sandwiches at a Japanese convenience store counter
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: This is not convenience store food
paragraphs: The first thing to understand about Japanese convenience stores 窶・konbini 窶・is that the food is genuinely good. Not 'good for a convenience store' good. Just good. The onigiri at 7-Eleven is made fresh every morning. The egg salad sandwich at Lawson is produced with a specific grade of bread and a specific ratio of egg to mayonnaise that has been optimized through decades of iteration. The hot foods at Family Mart are cooked to a standard that most fast-casual restaurants in other countries don't meet.

sections.heading: What konbini do beyond food
paragraphs: Konbini are everywhere. In Tokyo, you're never more than a three-minute walk from one. They're open 24 hours. They sell alcohol until midnight or all night depending on the prefecture. They have ATMs that accept foreign cards 窶・the 7-Bank machines at 7-Eleven are the most consistently reliable. They sell stamps, ship packages, pay utility bills, and print documents from a USB drive. They are infrastructure, and understanding them is understanding how Japan works.

sections.heading: Onigiri
paragraphs: What to eat. Onigiri are rice balls with a filling 窶・tuna mayo, pickled plum, salmon, bonito 窶・wrapped in a three-panel system that keeps the seaweed crispy until you open it. The opening system is printed on the packet with numbered arrows. Follow them. Don't try to unwrap it another way. You will end up with rice everywhere and no dignified explanation.

sections.heading: The egg salad sandwich
paragraphs: The egg salad sandwich (tamago sando) at 7-Eleven specifically has developed a following that crosses national lines. The egg is thick-cut and slightly sweet. The bread is soft in the particular way that soft white bread in Japan is soft, which is different from anywhere else and difficult to explain. Buy one on your first morning and the question of why people travel to Japan specifically for the food will answer itself.

sections.heading: Hot food
paragraphs: Hot food: the oden pot is a winter staple 窶・simmering broth with fish cakes, tofu, daikon, and boiled eggs. You pick items with tongs and the staff counts them into your bill. Steamed buns (nikuman) live in the hot case near the register and are excellent at 11pm when nothing else is open. The Family Mart chicken has been the subject of genuine national debate about whether it surpasses McDonald's. The answer depends on the outlet and the day.

sections.heading: Drinks
paragraphs: Drinks: canned coffee in Japan is a separate category of human achievement. Boss Coffee and the konbini own-brand cans have been developed through R&D cycles that most car companies would find excessive. Around ﾂ･120 a can, available cold or hot depending on the season. The hot canned coffee on a November morning in Tokyo is one of those sensory memories that travel writers try to describe and never quite manage.

sections.heading: Budget and how to order
paragraphs: Practical notes: budget ﾂ･500窶・00 for a full konbini meal. Pay at the register. Staff will ask 'atatame masuka?' (would you like this warmed up?) for eligible items. Yes is the answer. There will also be a question about a bag 窶・a small fee applies, but it's worth it. Don't eat inside the store if it can be avoided; there's usually a counter or seats outside.
