title: Tokyo's Kissaten: The Coffee Shops That Refuse to Change
excerpt: Somewhere between the third-wave cafﾃｩs and the chain stores, Tokyo's old coffee shops have been quietly waiting.
heroImageAlt: A dimly lit kissaten coffee shop interior with wooden counters and vintage cups
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What kissaten means
paragraphs: The word kissaten has no clean English translation. A coffee shop, technically. But that misses the point. A kissaten is a room where time moves differently 窶・where the owner has been making the same blend since 1978, where the cups are heavier than they need to be, where nobody is on a laptop, and where the ashtrays are still on the tables even if nobody uses them anymore.

sections.heading: Why they are invisible to visitors
paragraphs: Tokyo has thousands of them. Most are invisible to visitors because they don't appear on Instagram, they rarely have English menus, and the doors look like they open onto second-floor landings or basements with no visible signage. This is not an accident. Kissaten are not trying to attract new customers. They already have their customers, and those customers have been coming in for decades.

sections.heading: Where to find them
paragraphs: The ones worth finding are usually in older neighborhoods 窶・Koenji, Shimokitazawa, Yanaka, the parts of Shinjuku that Airbnb hasn't reached yet. They cluster near train stations that aren't on the tourist circuit. The right strategy is to walk until you see a menu in the window that was laminated in approximately 1990.

sections.heading: What you are drinking
paragraphs: What you're drinking is blend coffee 窶・filter or drip coffee made with a proprietary blend the owner has been refining for decades. This is not specialty coffee in the third-wave sense. It's not trying to taste of blueberries or dried apricot. It tastes of coffee, deeply, without fuss. Served black by default, with a small jug of cream on the side.

sections.heading: The morning set
paragraphs: Food at a kissaten tends to be one of two things: a morning set or toast. The morning set, usually available until 11am, is a cup of coffee plus a thick slice of toast with butter and jam 窶・sometimes with a small boiled egg on the side 窶・for ﾂ･400 to ﾂ･600. It is one of the great underrated breakfasts in the world, and it is not something you will find on any list of Tokyo's best breakfasts.

sections.heading: How to sit properly
paragraphs: Sitting in a kissaten requires a different pace. You don't order at a counter. You sit down, wait for the owner to come to you, and order quietly. Then you wait. The coffee takes longer to arrive than you'd expect, and you will feel, briefly, uncomfortable with the silence. That feeling passes. That's the point.

sections.heading: Three worth knowing in Tokyo
paragraphs: A few worth knowing in Tokyo: Cafﾃｩ de l'Ambre in Ginza (open since 1948; the owner worked there into his nineties), Chatei Hatou in Shibuya (precise and serious about the coffee), Kayaba Coffee in Yanaka (technically new, but in a building from 1938 that does the work). Don't go to all of them. Pick one, sit down properly, and stay for at least an hour.

sections.heading: What they represent
paragraphs: The reason kissaten deserve attention isn't nostalgia, exactly. It's that they represent a way of organizing time that has largely been optimized away. Nobody is tracking you. Nobody cares about table turnover. You ordered coffee and you're welcome to stay all morning. That this now feels unusual is its own kind of commentary.

title: Kanazawa: The City Most Japan Itineraries Skip 窶・and Shouldn't
excerpt: Three hours from Tokyo by bullet train. A fraction of Kyoto's crowds. Arguably better seafood. There is no good reason it's on so few itineraries.
heroImageAlt: Higashi Chaya geisha district in Kanazawa, wooden ochaya buildings lining a quiet street
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why Kanazawa is overlooked
paragraphs: Kanazawa is on the wrong side of Japan. That seems to be the only explanation for why it receives a third of Kyoto's visitor numbers while having a castle, a geisha district, one of Japan's three great landscape gardens, and what most serious food people consider the best seafood market outside of Tokyo.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: The bullet train from Tokyo (the Hokuriku Shinkansen, extended in 2015) takes 2.5 hours. From Kyoto it's 2.5 to 3 hours with a transfer. Neither journey is difficult. The problem is psychological 窶・most visitors arrive with a Tokyo-Kyoto-Osaka triangle already built into their itinerary, and Kanazawa sits outside of it, requiring a detour that most itineraries can't absorb.

sections.heading: Kenroku-en
paragraphs: Kenroku-en is the main draw, and correctly so. One of Japan's three great gardens 窶・a designation given by Edo-period landscape critics 窶・it was developed over two centuries by the ruling Maeda clan and covers 11.4 hectares. In cherry blossom season it's crowded. In late November, when the pine trees are wrapped in rope against the winter snow in a technique called yukizuri, it's extraordinary and very nearly empty.

sections.heading: Higashi Chaya
paragraphs: Higashi Chaya is Kanazawa's best-preserved geisha quarter and is smaller and less tourist-managed than Kyoto's Gion. Some of the ochaya (teahouses) are still operating. The street is wooden and narrow and hasn't changed structurally since the early 19th century. Come early morning or at dusk and it's yours.

sections.heading: The food
paragraphs: The food. Kanazawa sits on the Sea of Japan coast and receives fish from waters that don't reach the Pacific side in the same condition. The seafood at Omicho Market 窶・a covered market the size of a small neighborhood 窶・is some of the best in the country. In winter: crab, specifically the male snow crab called zuwaigani. In spring: firefly squid. Year-round: a sashimi set from any mid-range restaurant near the market will be better than most of what you'd eat in a Tokyo tourist district at twice the price.

sections.heading: Gold leaf and contemporary art
paragraphs: Kanazawa also has a serious craft tradition. Gold leaf production has been centered here since the Edo period 窶・99% of Japan's kinpaku (gold leaf) still comes from Kanazawa workshops. The '21st Century Museum of Contemporary Art' (designed by SANAA, open since 2004) is one of the better contemporary art museums in Japan and draws a local crowd, not just tourists.

sections.heading: Where to stay
paragraphs: Where to stay: the city center is small enough that almost anywhere is walkable. The area around Katamachi is convenient and has good restaurant density. Stay two nights minimum. The city rewards an unhurried second day in a way that a single overnight doesn't capture.

sections.heading: What Kanazawa has that Kyoto lost
paragraphs: What Kanazawa has that Kyoto has largely lost is the feeling of visiting somewhere people actually live. The geisha quarter isn't a theme park. The old town is genuinely old and genuinely in use. If you're looking for that 窶・and increasingly, it's what travelers say they want 窶・Kanazawa is close to the correct answer.

title: Tsukiji Outer Market: Where to Actually Eat (and What to Skip)
excerpt: The inner market moved to Toyosu in 2018. The outer market never moved and never needed to.
heroImageAlt: Fresh seafood stalls at Tsukiji outer market at early morning
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What stayed and what moved
paragraphs: In 2018, the famous wholesale fish market at Tsukiji relocated operations to Toyosu, a reclaimed island further east. Travel writers immediately started writing elegies for Tsukiji. What they missed is that the part most visitors cared about 窶・the outer market, with its 400-odd shops selling fresh seafood, street food, knives, and tamago-yaki 窶・never went anywhere and shows no signs of going anywhere.

sections.heading: When to arrive
paragraphs: The outer market opens early. Most stalls are running by 6am. The target arrival window is between 7am and 10am, when the selection is widest and the food is freshest. By noon, the best stalls will have sold out of the things worth eating. Come for breakfast. This is not negotiable.

sections.heading: Sushi
paragraphs: What to eat, in order of importance. First: sushi from one of the small counters in the side streets off the main market lane. These are eight-seat affairs with a glass case of fish in front. Point at what you want, sit down, eat it immediately. The fish was at Toyosu wholesale market this morning. A ﾂ･1,500窶・,000 set will be the best sushi you've had in your life, and if you don't believe that until you're eating it, that's fine.

sections.heading: Tamagoyaki
paragraphs: Second: tamagoyaki, the thick-rolled sweet omelette that is specific to the Tsukiji style. The most famous vendors are Tamagoyaki-ya Shouro and the stalls along the Namiyoke Shrine side. They sell slices from the pan on wooden sticks. The outside is slightly caramelized. The inside is custard-soft. It costs around ﾂ･200 and you will want two.

sections.heading: Ikura on rice
paragraphs: Third: ikura (salmon roe) on rice, from any stall that makes it fresh. The roe should be bright orange and very fresh 窶・the texture is different from what you've likely had before. A small bowl is ﾂ･500窶・00. It's one of those things that recalibrates your understanding of what fish can be.

sections.heading: What to skip
paragraphs: What to skip: the photographed seafood-on-skewer vendors near the main entrance that have been optimized for social media. The scallops are fine but priced for tourists, and the line is full of people taking the same photo. Walk past them, turn into the side streets, and find the stalls where nobody has their phone out.

sections.heading: The knife shops
paragraphs: The knife shops deserve a separate mention. Tsukiji has more professional knife dealers per square meter than anywhere outside of the knife-making cities. If you're planning to buy Japanese kitchen knives 窶・and you should, they are among the best things to bring home from Japan 窶・buy them here after handling them and talking to the vendor. The selection goes from ﾂ･3,000 santoku knives for everyday home cooking to ﾂ･80,000 custom yanagiba for the serious cook.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: Getting there: subway to Tsukijishijo (Oedo Line) or Tsukiji (Hibiya Line). Allow at least two hours. Three if you're serious about the knives.

title: How to Eat at an Izakaya Without Looking Completely Lost
excerpt: The curtain over the door. The laminated menu with pictures on maybe two pages. The small charge you didn't order. You'll be fine.
heroImageAlt: Inside a Tokyo izakaya with warm lighting, wooden tables and yakitori on the grill
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What an izakaya is
paragraphs: An izakaya is a Japanese pub 窶・not a restaurant, not a bar, but the thing that happens when the two concepts are combined without either one winning. You drink and eat in roughly equal measure. The food comes in small plates meant to be shared across the table. Nobody orders a main course. The lighting is low and the room is loud and you'll probably be sharing it with salarymen who have loosened their ties, which is always a good sign.

sections.heading: Ordering from the menu
paragraphs: Most izakaya have a printed menu and sometimes a laminated picture menu. Order from the picture menu if there is one. Point at what you want. Nobody will be offended. If there's no picture menu, say 'osusume wa nan desu ka?' 窶・what do you recommend? Someone will point at something, and whatever they point at will be edible.

sections.heading: The opening round
paragraphs: The standard opening order: a round of beers (nama biru, draft, or a sawa cocktail made from shochu and soda), plus two or three small dishes to start. Edamame, karaage (fried chicken), and potato salad are almost universally available and almost universally good. They are not exciting. They are correct. Order them while you figure out the rest of the menu.

sections.heading: Otoshi: the charge you did not order
paragraphs: The small charge that appears on your bill without explanation: it's called otoshi. Almost every izakaya charges a per-person seating fee of ﾂ･200窶・00, and in return brings a small snack you didn't order 窶・sometimes pickled vegetables, sometimes a small salad, occasionally something genuinely excellent. It's not a scam. It's standard. The bill is correct.

sections.heading: What to eat
paragraphs: Order drinks first, then food, then more drinks, then more food 窶・loosely. Good things to order beyond the basics: yakitori (grilled chicken on bamboo skewers 窶・order mixed, specify 'tare' for sweet soy glaze or 'shio' for salt), dashimaki tamago (rolled omelette made with dashi broth, usually soft and fragrant), and sashimi if the place looks like it takes its fish seriously, which you can usually tell from whether there are locals at the bar eating it.

sections.heading: Shochu and chuhai
paragraphs: Shochu is likely what you're drinking in the cocktails and don't know it. Distilled most commonly from sweet potato (imo-jochu) or barley (mugi-jochu), it's served over ice or mixed with soda. Gentler than whisky, it goes with everything on the menu. If someone offers chuhai, that's shochu with fruit soda 窶・the izakaya equivalent of a spritz, and perfectly fine.

sections.heading: Getting the bill
paragraphs: You leave when you've decided to leave. To get the bill, catch someone's eye and make a rectangular gesture with your index fingers 窶・this is the universal 'o-kaikei' signal and everyone will understand it immediately. The total will likely be split evenly if you're with a group. This is called warikan. Don't try to calculate individual shares. It goes against the spirit of the thing.

sections.heading: The golden rule
paragraphs: The golden rule: the more local and less translated the menu, the better the food. The izakaya in a residential neighborhood near a train station that isn't on any tourist map will beat the one with an English sign near the temples every time. Your hotel is on the tourist circuit. The izakaya should not be.

title: The IC Card Is the Best Thing in Japan. Here's How to Actually Use It.
excerpt: You can survive Tokyo's train network without one. You just won't want to.
heroImageAlt: Commuter tapping a Suica IC card at a Tokyo train station gate
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Get one at the airport
paragraphs: Before you leave the airport, find a ticket machine and get a Suica. Don't deliberate. Don't check whether you really need one. You need one.

sections.heading: What an IC card is
paragraphs: An IC card is a rechargeable transit card 窶・the same basic technology as London's Oyster or Hong Kong's Octopus 窶・except that in Japan it works on practically everything. Trains, subways, buses, ferries, taxis in major cities, convenience stores, vending machines, coin lockers, the self-checkout at Don Quijote at 2am. The list keeps growing.

sections.heading: Suica or ICOCA
paragraphs: Suica is issued by JR East and works in Tokyo and most of eastern Japan. ICOCA is the Kansai equivalent, issued by JR West, and works in Osaka, Kyoto, Kobe, and surrounding areas. The practical answer to which one you should get: they're fully interoperable. Get whichever one is available at the airport you land at. It genuinely doesn't matter.

sections.heading: Loading money
paragraphs: Loading money: find any green JR ticket machine, insert your card, press 'Charge' (there's an English option), and add ﾂ･1,000, ﾂ･2,000, or ﾂ･5,000. Keep ﾂ･2,000 or more on the card at all times. You don't want to be the person topping up at the fare gate while a queue builds behind you.

sections.heading: How tapping works
paragraphs: Tapping is the ritual that will define your first week. Tap the card on the reader as you enter the gate 窶・a satisfying beep. Tap again as you leave 窶・another beep, money deducted. A sad double-beep and a red light means insufficient balance. Step aside, top up at the nearest machine, which will be approximately five meters away.

sections.heading: Vending machines
paragraphs: One thing nobody mentions: the card works in vending machines too. Just tap the reader on the front panel. It's faster than coins, works every time, and makes carrying change feel archaic. After a week in Japan you'll find physical coins faintly offensive.

sections.heading: Apple Pay and Google Pay
paragraphs: Increasingly, you can also link Suica to Apple Pay or Google Pay. Setup takes about ten minutes and is worth doing if your phone supports it 窶・you can top up from the app, which means you never need to find a machine. The setup process is in English on both platforms.

sections.heading: When you leave Japan
paragraphs: When you leave Japan: keep the card. It doesn't expire, and having it ready for your next trip is genuinely convenient. If you want to return it, do so at a JR ticket office 窶・you'll get back your remaining balance minus a ﾂ･220 handling fee. If you have less than ﾂ･300 left, just keep it.

sections.heading: Why it matters
paragraphs: The larger point is this: the IC card makes it possible to navigate Tokyo's 130-plus stations within the Yamanote Line loop without ever buying a specific-destination ticket from a machine. That is not a trivial thing. It removes a class of friction that makes many transit systems 窶・especially large Japanese ones 窶・intimidating for first-time visitors. Get the card at the airport. Everything else is easier.

title: Japanese Whisky: What's Worth Buying and What's Just Marketing
excerpt: The golden age is over but the whisky is still exceptional. You just need to know what you're actually looking at.
heroImageAlt: Japanese whisky bottles displayed at a specialist liquor shop in Tokyo
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The golden age and the scarcity
paragraphs: Somewhere around 2014, the world decided Japanese whisky was the best in the world. Suntory's Yamazaki 12 Year won a prominent international award. Whisky writers made pilgrimages to Nikka's distillery in Hokkaido. By 2016 you couldn't find a bottle of Yamazaki Single Malt for under ﾂ･15,000 in a Tokyo duty-free. By 2020 you couldn't find it at all.

sections.heading: Why availability is slowly improving
paragraphs: The scarcity problem is real but overstated. What happened: global demand exploded, Japanese distilleries 窶・which had been producing at a domestic-consumption pace 窶・couldn't scale fast enough. The aged expressions ran dry. Secondary market prices went insane. But the distilleries kept working, and the whisky that was aging in 2016 and 2018 is now maturing. Availability is slowly improving.

sections.heading: What is genuinely worth buying
paragraphs: What's genuinely worth buying in 2026. Nikka From The Barrel: a blended whisky that consistently outperforms its price. Around ﾂ･3,000 at a convenience store if you're lucky enough to find it. Suntory Toki: an affordable blend designed for highballs 窶・order it at a bar before you commit to a bottle, which will take about one sip. Any single malt expression from Yamazaki or Yoichi that you can find at close to retail price rather than secondary market markup.

sections.heading: The highball format
paragraphs: The highball is the format. Japanese whisky culture is built around the whisky highball 窶・spirit over ice with soda, served in a tall frosted glass. It sounds humble. It isn't. Order one at any decent whisky bar before you start shopping; it will calibrate your sense of what you're buying.

sections.heading: Where to buy
paragraphs: Where to buy: department store basement floors (depachika) and specialist liquor stores have the best selection and prices. Airport duty-free is convenient but the offering has narrowed significantly since the shortage began. Avoid hotel gift shops. The markup is severe and the selection is whatever moves fastest with tourists.

sections.heading: On labeling
paragraphs: On labeling: 'Japanese whisky' now has legal standards, introduced in 2021. The spirit must be made in Japan from Japanese ingredients, malted and aged in Japan, and bottled in Japan. Before this regulation, some brands were blending imported Scottish or Canadian spirit and calling it Japanese. The rules now require transparency. If a bottle says 'blended' without a specific distillery name, read the fine print.

sections.heading: What you will actually find
paragraphs: What you're most likely to actually find and afford: Suntory Kakubin (yellow label, a classic blend, excellent for highballs, around ﾂ･1,200 at most liquor shops), Nikka Coffey Malt or Coffey Grain (made in a Coffey still, unusual and worth the ﾂ･7,000), and possibly Chichibu expressions from Ichiro's Malt if you're prepared to spend ﾂ･15,000 or more on something genuinely scarce.

sections.heading: One bottle to look for
paragraphs: One bottle to look for specifically: Mars Komagatake, from the highest-altitude distillery in Japan, located in Nagano. Production is limited and domestic allocation is small, but it shows up in specialist shops and occasionally at airport duty-free. It's not as famous as Yamazaki, but the gap in quality doesn't justify the gap in price and availability. Find it while that's still true.

title: Naoshima Is Japan's Art Island 窶・and Nobody Tells You How to Do It Right
excerpt: It's a real island with real people living on it. The art just happens to be everywhere.
heroImageAlt: A contemporary outdoor sculpture near the coast of Naoshima with the Seto Inland Sea behind
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What Naoshima is
paragraphs: Naoshima sits in the Seto Inland Sea between Okayama and Takamatsu, about an hour by ferry from either city. It's small enough to cross on a rented bicycle in an afternoon. The population is around 3,000. The island has more Monets per square kilometer than most museums in Europe, which is a fact that sounds invented but isn't.

sections.heading: How it became what it is
paragraphs: The backstory: in the 1980s and 90s, the Benesse Corporation commissioned architect Tadao Ando to build a museum on a largely depopulated island. What followed over the next three decades was a collaboration between art, architecture, and landscape that turned Naoshima into something that shouldn't quite work but absolutely does. It's been widely imitated. Nothing else is quite like it.

sections.heading: The Chichu Art Museum
paragraphs: The main venues. The Chichu Art Museum is underground 窶・no exterior visible from above, all natural light coming through skylights 窶・and contains exactly three artists: Monet (five late Water Lilies), James Turrell (light installations), and Walter De Maria. That's all. The ticket is ﾂ･2,100 and it is worth the trip from Tokyo to stand in the Monet room, which is a thing I don't say lightly.

sections.heading: The Art House Project
paragraphs: The Art House Project is what separates Naoshima from every other art destination in Japan. Artists were given abandoned houses in Honmura village and told to do what they wanted. James Turrell built a light installation into a farmhouse that you enter barefoot and lie on the floor of. Hiroshi Sugimoto turned a shrine into something else. These aren't galleries with labels and benches. They're encounters.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: Getting there from Tokyo is a half-day commitment minimum. Bullet train to Okayama, 20 minutes by local train to Uno port, 20-minute ferry to Naoshima. From Takamatsu it's about an hour by ferry. The journey filters out casual visitors, which is exactly why the island still has the quality it has.

sections.heading: Stay overnight
paragraphs: Stay overnight if you can. After the day-trip visitors leave on the last ferry, the island changes. The Art House Project buildings glow from inside. The grounds around Benesse House are open and quiet. You'll be one of a small number of people who aren't locals. This is an unusual experience in Japan and should be used properly 窶・walk around, eat at the small restaurant near the port, sit somewhere without your phone.

sections.heading: On bicycles
paragraphs: On bicycles: the island is hilly and summer heat is serious. Electric bikes are available at the ferry terminal and are not cheating. The climb between Honmura and the Benesse area in August on a regular bicycle is not a good time.

sections.heading: The right mindset
paragraphs: Naoshima rewards a particular mindset: willingness to be disoriented, to find things with no label, to sit with something that doesn't explain itself. If that's what you came to Japan for 窶・and increasingly, it is 窶・the island earns its reputation. If you're looking for informational plaques and a gift shop at every turn, the Benesse Foundation apologizes in advance.

title: The JR Pass: Who It's For, Who It Isn't, and How to Not Overpay
excerpt: The JR Pass is one of Japan's most famous travel products. It's also, for a lot of itineraries, a waste of money.
heroImageAlt: Shinkansen bullet train at a Japanese station platform
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The cost
paragraphs: The 7-day JR Pass costs ﾂ･50,000 (around $330 USD at current rates). For that price you get unlimited travel on most JR-operated trains, including the Shinkansen, for one week. The 14-day version is ﾂ･80,000. If your itinerary involves Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka, and Hiroshima in that sequence, the math works. If your itinerary is mostly in one city with a few day trips, it almost certainly doesn't.

sections.heading: Break-even: when the pass wins
paragraphs: The break-even calculation for the 7-day pass: a single Tokyo窶適yoto Shinkansen (Hikari) ticket is ﾂ･13,870 each way. Two of those is ﾂ･27,740. Add Kyoto窶徹saka (ﾂ･1,420), Osaka窶滴iroshima (ﾂ･5,720 each way), a couple of JR local trips, and you're somewhere near ﾂ･60,000. The pass wins. If you're only doing Tokyo窶適yoto round-trip with no other long-distance travel, a single round-trip costs ﾂ･27,740 against the pass price of ﾂ･50,000. You lose ﾂ･22,260.

sections.heading: Nozomi vs Hikari
paragraphs: The JR Pass does not cover the Nozomi or Mizuho (the fastest Shinkansen), only the Hikari and Kodama. Tokyo to Kyoto on a Hikari takes about 2h45m versus 2h15m on a Nozomi. This is not a big difference and most people don't notice it.

sections.heading: What the pass covers that people miss
paragraphs: What the pass does cover that people underestimate: the Narita Express (N'EX) from the airport, the local JR lines within Tokyo (which serve some neighborhoods that subway doesn't), the JR Haruka from Kansai Airport into Kyoto, and most Shinkansen within Kyushu (on the Kyushu JR Pass). If you're starting from Narita, the N'EX alone is ﾂ･3,070 to Shinjuku. Over seven days that's two trips or ﾂ･6,140 saved before you board a Shinkansen.

sections.heading: Alternatives to the JR Pass
paragraphs: Alternatives to consider: IC cards (Suica) cover everything within cities. Individual Shinkansen tickets bought in advance can be slightly discounted through JR's Ekinet system. The Osaka Amazing Pass and the Kyoto Sightseeing Card cover local transit within those cities and don't require a JR Pass to function alongside them.

sections.heading: If you have decided to buy one
paragraphs: If you've already decided to buy the pass: buy it before leaving your home country. It used to be only available abroad, and while you can now buy it in Japan, the domestic price is roughly 10窶・0% higher. Get it from JR's official website or an authorized travel agent in your country. Activate it at a JR office in any major station, not at the airport machines.

title: Where to Stay in Tokyo: Honest Assessments of Each Neighborhood
excerpt: Every first-time visitor books Shinjuku because someone told them to. Some of those people are right.
heroImageAlt: Aerial view of Tokyo at dusk showing the city lights
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Why neighborhood choice matters
paragraphs: Tokyo has 23 special wards and dozens of distinct neighborhoods, each with a different character and a different argument for why you should sleep there. The decision matters more than most hotel comparison sites suggest. An hour on the Yamanote Line from Ueno to Shibuya is not the same as a ten-minute walk from wherever you're standing. Where you sleep shapes what you do in the morning.

sections.heading: Shinjuku
paragraphs: Shinjuku: the right choice for first-timers who want maximum convenience. Shinjuku Station connects to almost everything. The west side has the skyscraper district and the Keio bus terminal for day trips. The east side has Kabukicho, Golden Gai, and Omoide Yokocho 窶・three different things to do at night. Hotels range from budget capsule options in the ﾂ･3,000窶・,000/night range to Park Hyatt at the top. The area is not charming, but it works.

sections.heading: Asakusa
paragraphs: Asakusa: the tourist district, with one of the city's best sightseeing densities within walking distance (Senso-ji, Nakamise, the Sumida River). The downside is that it's quieter outside tourist hours, transport options are limited compared to Shinjuku or Shibuya, and the neighborhood doesn't have the density of restaurants and bars that other areas do. Good for first-timers wanting concentrated sightseeing and a vaguely traditional atmosphere. Less good for eating out late or getting to Shibuya quickly.

sections.heading: Shibuya
paragraphs: Shibuya: better for people on a second or third visit. The neighborhood is in constant construction (it's been under renovation since 2012 and will remain so until approximately 2027). The crossing is iconic and worth seeing once; it's not worth basing a week around. The advantage is easy access to the private rail lines heading west 窶・Shimokitazawa on the Keio Inokashira Line, Nakameguro and Daikanyama within walking distance.

sections.heading: Ginza
paragraphs: Ginza: expensive, convenient, quiet at night. If your priorities are ease of getting around and you're not interested in nightlife, the business hotels here are excellent value relative to the address. Ginza Line goes directly to Shibuya. Walking distance to Tsukiji. Largely tourists and executives, which can feel sterile.

sections.heading: Ueno
paragraphs: Ueno: the most practical choice for travelers with a lot of luggage arriving into Ueno Station, which has direct Shinkansen connections from the north. The area around Ueno Park is dense with museums and cheap restaurants. It has none of Shinjuku's energy, but it has a functioning neighborhood character that Shinjuku lacks 窶・Yanaka and Nezu are walkable from here and are among the most interesting old-city districts in Tokyo.

sections.heading: The general rule
paragraphs: The general rule: within the Yamanote Line loop, you're never more than 20 minutes from anything important. The choice of neighborhood is about what you walk outside to in the morning, not about access. Pick based on the atmosphere you want for breakfast and evening, and trust the train system to handle the rest.

title: Japan's Four Major Ramen Styles: What They Are and Where to Actually Eat Them
excerpt: Ramen in Japan is not one food. Depending on where you are, the word covers a range of things that share almost nothing except noodles.
heroImageAlt: A bowl of Hakata tonkotsu ramen with chashu pork and soft-boiled egg
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Four styles, not one
paragraphs: The four canonical regional ramen styles in Japan are Sapporo (miso), Hakata (tonkotsu), Tokyo (shoyu), and Kitakata (shoyu with flat noodles). This is a simplification. Within each region there are dozens of substyles, proprietary broths, and variations by shop. But the four regional types give you a framework that is more useful than 'ramen is ramen.'

sections.heading: Hakata tonkotsu
paragraphs: Hakata ramen from Fukuoka is the style most likely to become your obsession. The broth is made from pork bones (tonkotsu) simmered for 12 to 18 hours until it turns milky white and rich enough to coat a spoon. The noodles are thin and straight, cooked harder than you'd expect. The correct serving size is small 窶・a half-portion (kogata) is standard, and you're expected to order kaedama (a noodle refill) into the remaining broth. Shops around Hakata Station and the yatai stalls along the Naka River are where to eat it. Budget ﾂ･700窶・,000.

sections.heading: Tokyo shoyu
paragraphs: Tokyo shoyu ramen is the gentler style. Chicken or pork-based broth seasoned with soy sauce, usually clearer than Hakata broth, with medium wavy noodles. The toppings are what distinguish one Tokyo shop from another: chashu pork, a soft-boiled egg marinated in soy and mirin, bamboo shoots, nori. The shop Fuunji in Shinjuku makes a tsukemen (dipping ramen) version that requires arriving at 11am or accepting a 45-minute wait.

sections.heading: Sapporo miso
paragraphs: Sapporo miso ramen is the winter version 窶・a heavier broth with miso paste stirred in, often with butter, sweet corn, and bean sprouts on top. It makes sense in Hokkaido in November in a way it doesn't in Tokyo in August. The Susukino neighborhood in Sapporo has the highest concentration of shops worth trying. Expect to spend ﾂ･900窶・,200.

sections.heading: How to order
paragraphs: How to order at a ramen shop: most have ticket vending machines at the entrance. Put in your money, select your bowl, take the ticket, and hand it to staff when you sit. If you want a soft-boiled egg (onsen tamago or ajitama), it's usually a separate button. Noodle firmness (kata for firm, yawarakai for soft) and richness (koi for heavy, asa for light) can be specified at some shops. Do not over-specify at your first visit. Order the standard and learn what you're tasting before you start adjusting.

title: How to Watch a Sumo Tournament Without Watching It on TV
excerpt: Six tournaments a year, three of them in Tokyo. Tickets go on sale months in advance and sell out. Here's how to actually get in.
heroImageAlt: Two sumo wrestlers facing off in the dohyo ring at Ryogoku Kokugikan
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Tournament schedule and cities
paragraphs: Sumo holds six official tournaments (basho) per year, each lasting 15 days. The January, May, and September basho are in Tokyo at Ryogoku Kokugikan. The March basho is in Osaka, July in Nagoya, and November in Fukuoka. If you're planning a trip around attending a tournament, knowing this schedule is step one. January and September in Tokyo are typically the most accessible for international visitors because they don't overlap with Golden Week or summer school holidays.

sections.heading: Getting tickets
paragraphs: Getting tickets: the Japan Sumo Association sells official tickets at Ticket Pia starting roughly two months before the tournament. The best seats (masu-seki, the box seats on the ground floor) sell out in days. What's available to visitors with short notice: elevated arena seats (isu-seki), which start around ﾂ･3,800 for the upper tiers, and same-day door tickets (ﾂ･2,200 for the cheap seats) that go on sale at the venue from 8am. The door ticket strategy works 窶・arrive at Ryogoku at 7:30am and you will get in.

sections.heading: What you are watching
paragraphs: What you're watching: sumo matches are short. The actual bout can last anywhere from two seconds to two minutes. The bulk of the time in a sumo tournament is ritual 窶・the ring-entering ceremony (dohyo-iri) for the highest-ranked wrestlers, salt throwing, the slow psychological confrontation before the wrestlers crouch and charge. Understanding that the ceremony is the event, not the prelude to it, changes everything about how to watch.

sections.heading: When to arrive
paragraphs: Timing: the day begins at 8am with the lowest-ranked wrestlers, and the audience trickles in as the day progresses. The top-division matches (makuuchi) begin around 3pm and end around 6pm. Most visitors arrive around 2pm, which is correct. The atmosphere builds through the afternoon. The final few bouts of the day 窶・particularly if a yokozuna or ozeki is involved in a high-stakes match 窶・generate a noise level that is genuinely startling.

sections.heading: Food and drink at Kokugikan
paragraphs: What to eat and drink at the venue: the basement of Kokugikan has chankonabe, the stew traditionally eaten by sumo wrestlers. It is not a gimmick. It's good stew, with chicken and vegetables in a clear dashi broth, and it costs around ﾂ･1,000. There are also yakitori stalls and beer vendors. Food at the masu boxes is served in bento-style sets and can be ordered in advance with tickets.

sections.heading: What surprises you in person
paragraphs: The wrestlers are larger than you expect. The ring is smaller than you expect. The speed of the bouts is faster than you expect. These three surprises reliably improve the live experience over whatever you were imagining from television coverage.

title: The Onsen Rules Nobody Bothers to Tell You
excerpt: The water is shared. The silence is expected. The tattoo policy is more complicated than you've been told.
heroImageAlt: Outdoor rotenburo onsen with steam rising over a stone tub surrounded by bamboo
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The rule everyone needs to know
paragraphs: The fundamental rule of a Japanese onsen or public bath is that you wash before you enter the communal water. This is not optional and not a suggestion. There are shower stations around the perimeter 窶・a stool, a handheld showerhead, shampoo and conditioner in dispensers. You sit on the stool, rinse yourself thoroughly, wash your hair if you're going in with your head back, and then you may enter the bath. Anyone who walks past the shower stations and steps directly into the communal water has misunderstood what they're doing here.

sections.heading: What you bring in
paragraphs: What you bring into the bath: nothing, essentially. A small towel is acceptable if kept out of the water 窶・fold it and put it on your head, or leave it at the edge of the tub. Soap goes at the shower station, not into the bath. Your phone doesn't come into the onsen area at all. This is enforced in most facilities and enforced seriously.

sections.heading: Tattoos: the real policy
paragraphs: Tattoos: the official policy at most traditional onsen is no tattoos, a rule that dates to historical associations between tattoos and organized crime. Enforcement varies significantly. Large resort onsen tend to be stricter; smaller neighborhood sento (public baths) are often more pragmatic. Private family baths (kashikiri buro), bookable by the hour at many ryokan and onsen hotels, have no policy because there are no other guests. If you have visible tattoos and want to use a communal onsen without anxiety, look for facilities with explicit tattoo-friendly policies 窶・they exist and are increasingly common.

sections.heading: Temperature
paragraphs: Temperature: onsen run hot. 40窶・2ﾂｰC is standard. 44ﾂｰC exists. The first immersion will feel excessive. Give yourself 30 seconds to acclimatize before concluding you can't handle it. The correct approach is to enter slowly, sit still, and breathe steadily. You will feel fine within a minute. If you feel dizzy or lightheaded at any point, get out. This is normal and the fault of the heat, not weakness.

sections.heading: Rotating between baths
paragraphs: Rotating baths and timing: most onsen have multiple baths 窶・an indoor main bath, possibly an outdoor rotenburo, a cold plunge bath, a sauna, a carbonated bath. The protocol is to move between them over 30窶・0 minutes rather than sitting in one until you've cooked. Cold plunge (mizu buro) after a hot soak is optional but converts most people the first time they try it. The contrast is the point.

sections.heading: After the onsen
paragraphs: After the bath: dry off in the changing room before going anywhere else. The post-onsen state 窶・warm, slightly light-headed, skin strangely soft 窶・is best enjoyed horizontal for 15 minutes. Drink water. Many ryokan leave cold water or barley tea in the changing room for this purpose. The evening onsen before bed followed by eight hours of sleep in a cool room is, empirically, the best sleep you will have all year.

title: Don Quijote: What to Buy, What to Skip, and How to Survive the Store
excerpt: Don Quijote is five floors of everything at prices that make no sense. The key is knowing what you came for before you walk in.
heroImageAlt: A colorful Japanese discount store exterior at night with bright signs in Tokyo
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What Don Quijote is
paragraphs: Don Quijote 窶・called Donki by everyone who shops there 窶・is a discount variety store that operates on the principle that if you put enough products in a small enough space at a low enough price, people will buy things they didn't know they needed. This works. The stores are open until 5am. The in-store music is a jingle that plays on loop. The lighting is aggressive. It is not a relaxing shopping experience. It is an effective one.

sections.heading: Skincare and cosmetics
paragraphs: What to buy. Skincare and cosmetics: Donki carries Japanese drugstore brands (Hada Labo, Kose, Shiseido) and some Korean imports at prices below anywhere else you'll find them in Japan. The Hada Labo Gokujyun moisturizing lotion (170ml, around ﾂ･500) is the specific item that beauty content creators have been writing about for years, and the Donki price is genuinely the best in Tokyo. Load up.

sections.heading: Japanese snacks
paragraphs: Japanese snacks: the basement or ground floor of most Donki has a food section with Kit Kat flavors, Pocky, regional chips, and gift-packaged snack sets. If you're buying snacks to take home, this is more convenient than tracking down a specialty snack shop and often cheaper. Green tea Kit Kat in the standard retail package at around ﾂ･300 a box is a reasonable gift that travels well.

sections.heading: Electronics and gadgets
paragraphs: Electronics and gadgets: the electronics section has genuine deals on portable chargers, travel adapters, and USB cables. Don't buy anything complex 窶・laptops and cameras here require Japanese region settings and Japanese warranties. The accessories are fine.

sections.heading: What to skip
paragraphs: What to skip: branded luxury goods in the glass cases near the register. These are legitimate products but the selection is limited and the prices aren't as competitive as the rest of the store. Perfumes are hit or miss. For Japanese whisky, sake, or shochu worth buying, go to a dedicated liquor store.

sections.heading: Navigation
paragraphs: Navigation: the store layout is deliberately non-intuitive. Signage exists but products are stacked floor-to-ceiling in a way that turns navigation into something between a maze and an archaeological dig. Accept this. Give yourself at least 90 minutes for your first visit. Don't try to optimize; let the store find you things. The Shinjuku and Shibuya branches are the largest and most complete. The Akihabara branch is useful if you want the electronics section without the full Donki experience.

title: Hiroshima Is More Than the Peace Park 窶・And the Peace Park Is More Than You Think
excerpt: People visit Hiroshima for one reason. Most leave having found three or four others.
heroImageAlt: The Genbaku Dome atomic bomb memorial in Hiroshima beside the river
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The Peace Memorial Museum
paragraphs: The Peace Memorial Park and Museum is why most people come to Hiroshima, and it should be. The museum documents what happened on August 6, 1945 with a directness and specificity that other memorial museums often avoid 窶・personal objects, shadows burned into stone, recorded testimony. The Children's Peace Monument, the Genbaku Dome, the Flame of Peace that has burned continuously since 1964 and is meant to burn until all nuclear weapons are gone. Budget two to three hours, not one.

sections.heading: Hiroshima beyond the park
paragraphs: What you'll find beyond the memorial. Hiroshima has one of Japan's most interesting covered shotengai (shopping arcade) districts, centered around Hondori and its side streets. It's genuinely local 窶・not a tourist market, but the arcade where people in Hiroshima buy clothes and eat lunch. The okonomiyaki restaurants here are a reason to visit Hiroshima independent of the memorial.

sections.heading: Hiroshima okonomiyaki
paragraphs: Hiroshima okonomiyaki is different from Osaka okonomiyaki. The Osaka version mixes everything together; the Hiroshima version layers ingredients 窶・batter, bean sprouts, cabbage, noodles (yes, noodles), pork, egg 窶・in sequence on a flat griddle. The result is denser and more substantial than the Osaka style. The specific place for it is Okonomimura, a three-floor building near the Hondori arcade with around 25 individual shops each making their version. Pick one that looks busy. Budget ﾂ･1,000窶・,400.

sections.heading: Miyajima Island
paragraphs: Miyajima Island: a 10-minute ferry from Hiroshima port (covered by the JR Pass). The vermilion torii gate of Itsukushima Shrine, standing in the sea at high tide, is one of those images that is on every Japan poster and still manages to be more striking in person than in photographs. Walk past the shrine to the hills 窶・Daishoin Temple and the path up to Mt. Misen take you away from the day-trippers within 20 minutes. The island also has deer, which are smaller and less aggressive than Nara's but will absolutely eat your map.

sections.heading: How long to stay
paragraphs: Timing: Hiroshima is a half-day to full-day stop on the standard Tokyo窶適yoto窶徹saka route (Shinkansen from Shin-Osaka takes about 85 minutes). A full day allows the museum, Hondori, lunch, and a Miyajima trip. Staying overnight allows a more complete Miyajima experience and access to the city at night, when the riverfront around the Motoyasu-gawa is worth walking.

title: Autumn Leaves in Japan: Where to Go and When the Timing Actually Works
excerpt: Koyo 窶・autumn foliage 窶・is not a fixed event. It starts in Hokkaido in September and ends in Kyushu in December. The timing requires a decision.
heroImageAlt: Maple trees in full autumn colour reflected in a garden pond in Kyoto
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: When koyo happens where
paragraphs: Japan's autumn color season (koyo) moves southward and downhill from September through late November. Hokkaido peaks first 窶・late September to mid-October 窶・while central Honshu (Nikko, the Japanese Alps, Kyoto) peaks in early to mid-November. Kyushu and the warmer lowlands finish last, sometimes into December. 'When is the best time for autumn leaves?' depends entirely on where you're going, and the answer shifts by two to four weeks depending on whether you're in the mountains or at sea level.

sections.heading: Kyoto in November
paragraphs: Kyoto's koyo season typically peaks in the second and third weeks of November. The problem: so does Kyoto's tourist density. Tenryu-ji in Arashiyama, Eikan-do in Nanzen-ji, the garden at Tofuku-ji 窶・all of these are spectacular and all of them will be packed. The answer is not to avoid them (the crowds exist because the places are worth it) but to arrange your visit around timing. These gardens look different at 7:30am than at 11am. The light is better and the ratio of leaves to people is significantly higher.

sections.heading: Nikko: the underrated option
paragraphs: The underrated alternative: Nikko, two hours by express from Ueno in Tokyo. The cryptomeria avenue leading to Tosho-gu Shrine, surrounded by beech and maple turning gold and orange, is one of the better koyo experiences in Japan and receives a fraction of Kyoto's November visitors. The Irohazaka switchback road to Lake Chuzenji is the specific spot 窶・worth the drive if you have transport, or a bus if you don't.

sections.heading: The Japanese Alps
paragraphs: The Japanese Alps for serious foliage: Kamikochi (closed after mid-November), Shirakawa-go, and the Tateyama Kurobe Alpine Route offer high-altitude autumn that peaks in October and is genuinely spectacular. Shirakawa-go's thatched-roof farmhouses surrounded by autumn maples is the kind of composition that doesn't require a good photographer 窶・the scene does the work.

sections.heading: Rain and overcast days
paragraphs: What nobody says: rain makes autumn foliage better. The colors are more saturated. The light through wet maple leaves is different from the light through dry ones. An overcast day in Kyoto in November is not a failed day; it's a different photograph and a quieter street. Adjust expectations and pack accordingly.

sections.heading: The practical window
paragraphs: The practical window: if you have flexibility in your travel dates, aim for November 10窶・0 for central Honshu. Before that, the color is incomplete. After that, the leaves have begun to fall and you're watching the end of something. The narrow peak is what makes koyo worth planning around.

title: Beppu: The Onsen City That's Stranger and Better Than You Expect
excerpt: Beppu produces more geothermal steam per day than anywhere in Japan and possibly anywhere outside Iceland. The city treats this as completely normal.
heroImageAlt: Blue-green geothermal pool steaming at Umi Jigoku in Beppu
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What Beppu is
paragraphs: Beppu is on the northeast coast of Kyushu, about an hour and a half from Fukuoka by limited express train. The city has eight distinct onsen zones (onsen hatto), each with different mineral compositions and water colors 窶・clear, milky white, reddish brown, and the vivid cobalt blue of the Kannawa area. The steam rising off the streets in certain neighborhoods on cool mornings makes it look like the city is slowly cooking from below, which is more or less accurate.

sections.heading: The Hell Tour
paragraphs: The Jigoku Meguri 窶・the Hell Tour 窶・is the tourist attraction: seven pools of geothermally active water that can't be bathed in because they're too hot (98ﾂｰC in some cases) or too acidic. The pools are genuinely remarkable. Umi Jigoku (Sea Hell) is deep blue and appears to be 200 meters wide. Chinoike Jigoku (Blood Pond Hell) is exactly as red as it sounds. Oniyama Jigoku contains a crocodile enclosure because the groundwater is warm enough to maintain tropical reptiles year-round. A circuit ticket covers all seven for ﾂ･2,200. It's worth it.

sections.heading: Bathing options
paragraphs: The actual bathing options are the reason to stay overnight. Kannawa and the older neighborhood onsens offer public baths (kotoyu) for as little as ﾂ･100窶・00 窶・coin-operated, simple tiled rooms, water that does things to your skin that hotel onsen doesn't quite replicate. Hyotan Onsen near Kannawa has eight distinct pools and won a prestigious onsen award; ﾂ･1,300 for full access, open until 1am.

sections.heading: Sand bathing
paragraphs: Sand bathing: Takegawara Onsen in central Beppu offers sunayu 窶・you lie in a shallow pit and staff bury you in geothermally heated sand. The sand temperature is around 50ﾂｰC, which sounds alarming and is instead comfortable in a way that's difficult to explain until you've experienced it. Fifteen minutes in the sand is roughly equivalent to an hour in a conventional bath in terms of post-session relaxation. ﾂ･1,500, no appointment needed.

sections.heading: Getting there and where to stay
paragraphs: Getting there and where to stay: the Sonic or Sonata limited express from Hakata (Fukuoka) takes 1h45m窶・h and costs around ﾂ･4,500 (or covered by JR Pass). The city is small enough that the main onsen areas are walkable from the station or a ﾂ･700 taxi ride. Ryokan here are genuinely affordable compared to Hakone 窶・ﾂ･10,000窶・5,000 per person including dinner and breakfast is achievable. Beppu makes a strong case for being the most accessible serious onsen town in Japan.

title: Japanese Stationery Is Worth the Suitcase Space
excerpt: Pens, notebooks, tape, paper clips. Japan has turned the mundane into an argument for why other countries aren't trying hard enough.
heroImageAlt: Rows of colorful washi tape rolls and stationery at a Japanese stationery shop
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The Pilot Hi-Tec-C case for Japanese stationery
paragraphs: The Pilot Hi-Tec-C is a 0.3mm gel pen that costs ﾂ･220 at any stationery shop in Japan and writes with a precision that no comparable pen in Europe or North America has matched at any price. This is not enthusiasm. It is a factual statement about the difference between the Japanese stationery industry and everyone else's, and it is the reason you should set aside half a day for stationery shopping.

sections.heading: The main chains
paragraphs: The two main chains: Itoya in Ginza (12 floors, a serious institutional stationery store that's been operating since 1904) and Loft (department store format, wide selection, multiple Tokyo locations). Itoya is for people who want to be deliberate about what they buy and spend two hours doing it. Loft is for people who want variety and efficiency. Tokyu Hands, now rebranded as Hands, has the broadest range of craft and stationery supplies.

sections.heading: Hobonichi planners and Midori notebooks
paragraphs: What to buy. Hobonichi planners: a cult Japanese planner published by the company that made Mother 2. Available in several formats (Techo, Weeks, Cousin); the standard Techo is an A6 daily planner printed on Tomoe River paper, which is 52gsm and shows through ink without bleeding. ﾂ･3,300 at official stores. Midori MD paper products: notebooks and pads using MD paper, which accepts fountain pen ink unusually well. The A5 blank notebook is ﾂ･900.

sections.heading: MT masking tape
paragraphs: MT masking tape: washi tape in a range of patterns and widths, sold individually at ﾂ･200窶・00 a roll. The flagship store in Ginza (MT Lab) has several hundred patterns. The tape is removable, repositionable, and takes ink without smearing 窶・it has acquired a following among journalers and planners globally that has made the brand an export success, though the selection in Japan is larger and cheaper than anywhere else.

sections.heading: Pens worth buying
paragraphs: For pens: the Uni-ball One (gel ink, available in 0.38mm and 0.5mm, multiple colors, ﾂ･300) and the Zebra Mildliner (a dual-tip highlighter with pastel tones that don't cause eye fatigue) are the two most commonly recommended items by people who work with paper professionally. Both are widely available and inexpensive enough to buy in quantity.

sections.heading: The suitcase argument
paragraphs: Suitcase space: stationery is light and flat. A full day's shopping fits in a document bag. The Pilot Hi-Tec-C pens are ﾂ･220 each and weigh nothing. The Hobonichi is the same size as a passport. This is one of the gift categories where there's no excuse not to buy for everyone you know.

title: Eating at a Sushi Counter: What to Expect and How to Not Ruin It
excerpt: The sushi counter is not intimidating once you understand that there are three rules, they're easy, and the chef wants you to enjoy it.
heroImageAlt: A sushi chef preparing nigiri behind a counter with fresh fish displayed in a glass case
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: Counter sushi vs conveyor belt
paragraphs: Counter sushi (omakase or a la carte) is different from kaiten (conveyor belt) in format, price, and the nature of the relationship between you and the chef. At the counter, there are usually 8窶・2 seats and a clear glass case of today's fish. You either order a set omakase (chef's choice, typically ﾂ･5,000窶・0,000 per person) or point at items in the case and they're made in front of you. The fish was at the market this morning. The rice is slightly warm. The whole operation is built around immediacy.

sections.heading: Three rules
paragraphs: Three rules. One: eat the sushi as soon as it's placed in front of you. The rice is seasoned and tempered for eating now, not in three minutes. Sushi that sits at room temperature changes. Two: it is acceptable to eat nigiri with your fingers. This is actually the traditional method. Using chopsticks risks the topping sliding off and is optional. Three: dip fish-side down into soy sauce, not rice-side down (soy sauce in the rice makes it fall apart and oversalts the bite).

sections.heading: What to order
paragraphs: What to order if you can. Tuna in its hierarchy: chutoro (medium fatty tuna) over maguro (lean tuna) over otoro (full fatty tuna) 窶・not because otoro isn't extraordinary, but because it's overwhelming in large quantities. The best first piece at a sushi counter is usually whatever the chef puts in front of you first, which is often a lighter white fish like hirame (flounder) or suzuki (sea bass) designed to calibrate your palate.

sections.heading: Ginger and wasabi
paragraphs: Ginger (gari) is a palate cleanser, eaten between pieces, not on top of them. Wasabi is incorporated into the sushi by the chef at a traditional counter 窶・additional wasabi on the side is available but signals that you found the amount insufficient. This is fine. Ask.

sections.heading: Budget guidance
paragraphs: Budget guidance: the most common misconception about counter sushi in Japan is that it's prohibitively expensive. At a mid-range counter in Tokyo away from Ginza, ﾂ･3,000窶・,000 buys a satisfying a la carte meal of 8窶・0 pieces plus tea. The ﾂ･20,000+ experience exists and is extraordinary, but it's not the entry point. Lunch sets at sushi restaurants often offer the same fish as dinner at 30窶・0% lower prices. Tsukiji Outer Market, Ueno, and the side streets of Shinjuku have the highest density of good-value options.

title: Nara Beyond the Deer: What to Do After the First Hour
excerpt: Yes, the deer are free-roaming and yes, they will bow to get crackers from you. This takes about 40 minutes. Nara has a full day's worth of things after that.
heroImageAlt: Sika deer roaming free in Nara Park with Todai-ji temple visible through the trees
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The deer
paragraphs: Nara Park has approximately 1,200 sika deer that have been designated national treasures and have, over generations of contact with tourists, learned that a particular bowing gesture triggers cracker-giving. You can buy shika-senbei (deer crackers) from vendors at ﾂ･200 a pack. The deer will absolutely approach you. Some will grab your bag. One will probably bite you lightly on the jacket. This is fine and expected and takes under an hour before it becomes routine. The question is what you do with the rest of the day.

sections.heading: Todai-ji
paragraphs: Todai-ji Temple: the largest wooden building in the world and home to a 15-meter bronze Buddha (the Nara Daibutsu) that has been sitting in approximately the same position since 752 AD. Entrance is ﾂ･600. The scale is genuinely difficult to convey 窶・the Buddha's nostril is the size of a small door, and there is a pillar inside the building with a hole at the base that's the same diameter as the nostril, which children (and optimistic adults) attempt to crawl through as a matter of ritual. Budget 45 minutes.

sections.heading: Kasuga Taisha
paragraphs: Kasuga Taisha: a Shinto shrine established in 768 AD, famous for its 3,000 stone and bronze lanterns that are lit twice a year (Setsubun in February and Obon in August). The path through the cedar forest to reach it is as good as the shrine itself 窶・moss-covered stone lanterns lining the approach, deer wandering between the cedars. Entry to the outer grounds is free; the inner precinct is ﾂ･500.

sections.heading: Naramachi
paragraphs: Naramachi: the preserved merchant town directly south of Nara Park. Narrow alleys, machiya townhouses, textile shops, sake breweries, and craft cafﾃｩs 窶・the kind of neighborhood that was here 200 years ago and has been left largely intact. It's quieter than the park area and full of places to eat. Nakatanidou mochi shop has a famous kinako mochi (pounded rice cake) that queues extend to the street; the Kasugayama area has older restaurants with proper set lunch menus.

sections.heading: Getting there and timing
paragraphs: Nara is 45 minutes from Kyoto by JR (ﾂ･730 or JR Pass) or about the same on the Kintetsu Line. It works as a half-day or full-day trip from either Kyoto or Osaka. The main park and Todai-ji can be crowded in the morning; the deer and crowds thin significantly after 3pm. If you're coming on a weekday and can arrive at 8:30am, the park before the day-trippers show up is a genuinely different experience.

title: Golden Week: What It Is, Why It Will Destroy Your Trip, and When to Go Instead
excerpt: Golden Week runs from late April to early May. If your Japan trip overlaps with it, you have two options: embrace the chaos or reschedule.
heroImageAlt: Crowded hanami cherry blossom viewing in Ueno Park during Golden Week
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What Golden Week is
paragraphs: Golden Week is Japan's largest consecutive holiday period 窶・a cluster of four national holidays between April 29 and May 5 that most companies treat as a full week off. Around 20 million Japanese people travel domestically during this window. International visitors add to this. The result is that every popular destination, every Shinkansen, every tourist attraction, and every hotel in Japan is at or near maximum capacity simultaneously.

sections.heading: What actually happens
paragraphs: What actually happens: the Tokaido Shinkansen from Tokyo to Osaka sells out weeks in advance and runs extra services that also sell out. Popular ryokan are fully booked four to six months ahead. The queue for Fushimi Inari in Kyoto, normally manageable at 7am, is significant by 8am. Hakone accommodation triples in price. Narita arrivals and departures are maximally busy. None of this is impossible to navigate; it's just not the Japan trip most people imagine.

sections.heading: If you are already booked
paragraphs: If you're already booked during Golden Week: lean into it. Go to places that are interesting specifically when full of Japanese people rather than tourists. City parks during Golden Week 窶・Yoyogi in Tokyo, Maruyama in Kyoto 窶・are excellent: families with picnic setups, food stalls, outdoor performances, a festive atmosphere that you don't get in October. The experience is genuinely different rather than categorically worse.

sections.heading: Shinkansen strategy
paragraphs: The Shinkansen strategy: if you have a JR Pass, reserved seats on Shinkansen are essential. Reserve them the moment you activate your pass. Non-reserved cars during Golden Week are standing-room only from Tokyo to Kyoto. If you don't have a JR Pass, buy Shinkansen tickets as far in advance as possible through Ekinet or at any JR ticket office.

sections.heading: When to go instead
paragraphs: When to go instead: late October and early November for autumn color with reasonable crowds. January for minimum tourists and maximum winter onsen value. Late September, after the summer humidity breaks. The weeks immediately after Golden Week ends (May 10窶・5) are excellent 窶・the weather is mild, crowds have dispersed, and hotel rates drop back to normal.

title: What to Buy When You Buy Japanese Sake
excerpt: Sake is rice wine in the same way that champagne is grape juice. The category is more complex than most people expect, and the range of quality is enormous.
heroImageAlt: Rows of sake bottles at a specialist sake shop in Japan
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The two main variables
paragraphs: Sake is fermented rice, water, yeast, and koji mold. The main variables: how much the rice has been polished before brewing (the seimaibuai, expressed as a percentage of the grain remaining), and whether distilled alcohol has been added. These two factors create the main classification categories that appear on labels and that are worth understanding before you buy anything.

sections.heading: Classifications that matter
paragraphs: The classifications that matter. Junmai: pure rice sake, no added alcohol. Honjozo: a small amount of distilled alcohol added, which lightens the sake and adds aroma. Ginjo: rice polished to at least 60% (40% milled away), more aromatic. Daiginjo: polished to at least 50%, the top tier, typically the most delicate and expensive. Combine these: Junmai Daiginjo is pure rice, top-tier polish, usually the premium product. Futsushu is table sake 窶・no premium designation, reasonable for everyday drinking.

sections.heading: What to actually buy
paragraphs: What to actually buy. For a gift or something to drink on the trip: a Junmai Ginjo or Junmai Daiginjo from a mid-sized regional brewery priced between ﾂ･2,000 and ﾂ･5,000 (720ml). The big names (Dassai, Kubota, Hakkaisan) are reliably good and widely available in department stores and specialist shops; they became famous because they're excellent. Dassai 23 (polished to 23% remaining) is exceptional and expensive (around ﾂ･5,000 for 300ml); Dassai 39 is similar in character at two-thirds the price.

sections.heading: Nigori sake
paragraphs: Nigori sake (unfiltered, cloudy, with rice sediment) is best drunk cold and fresh 窶・it doesn't travel as well. If you're drinking in Japan, try it; if you're buying to bring home, stick to clear filtered sake in sealed bottles.

sections.heading: Where to buy
paragraphs: Where to buy: department store basement floors (depachika) have the best curated range and staff who can explain what they're selling. Specialist sake shops (sakaya) exist in most cities 窶・Tokyo's Hasegawa Saketen chain is a reliable option with English-speaking staff at some locations. Avoid convenience store sake for gifts; it's functional but unmemorable.

sections.heading: Storage and shelf life
paragraphs: Storage and shelf life: premium sake is best kept refrigerated and consumed within a few months of purchase. If you're transporting it home, keep it cold where possible and drink it within six months. Nigori in particular degrades faster. Honjozo and Futsushu are more forgiving. Buy what you can drink.

title: Hokkaido in July: Japan's Best-Kept Summer Secret
excerpt: Tokyo in July is 35 degrees and 90% humidity. Hokkaido in July is 22 degrees and clear. These two facts explain a lot.
heroImageAlt: Lavender fields in full bloom at Farm Tomita in Furano, Hokkaido
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The climate case
paragraphs: Hokkaido is Japan's northernmost island and its least densely populated. The summer climate is a direct consequence of this geography: while central Honshu is enduring a summer that most temperate-climate visitors find difficult, Hokkaido is experiencing something close to a Northern European July. Average temperatures in Sapporo in July run 22窶・5ﾂｰC, with lower humidity. It's possible to walk outside without immediately sweating through your clothes. For visitors arriving from humid climates, this discovery is approximately life-changing.

sections.heading: Lavender fields in Furano
paragraphs: The lavender fields of Furano are the most photographed image of Hokkaido summer: long rows of purple lavender against a backdrop of hills and clouds, peaking in early to mid-July. Farm Tomita in Furano is the most famous, and is legitimately beautiful despite being a tourist operation. Come early morning. The crowds arrive by 10am. The lavender at 7am with mist still on the hills is the real version of the photograph you've seen.

sections.heading: Biei and the Panorama Road
paragraphs: Biei: a small town 20 kilometers north of Furano with rolling farmland 窶・patchwork fields of potato, wheat, sunflower, and canola 窶・that attracts photographers specifically for the quality of the light in summer. Renting a bicycle and spending the morning on the Panorama Road is the correct approach. No specific sight to reach; the road and the fields are the destination.

sections.heading: Sapporo
paragraphs: Sapporo: the capital and the city that functions as the base. The Odori Park beer garden runs all summer (July窶鄭ugust) 窶・rows of tables under the elm trees with brewery stalls from Sapporo, Asahi, Kirin, and Suntory running simultaneously. This is worth a single evening on its own terms. Soup curry, a Sapporo invention, is served in most restaurants here and is one of those regional dishes that makes no sense until you've eaten it.

sections.heading: Daisetsuzan National Park
paragraphs: The Daisetsuzan National Park: the largest national park in Japan, an alpine region accessible from multiple trailheads. The ropeway at Asahidake gives access to volcanic terrain above 1,500 meters where the hiking is excellent and the views extend across the island. A serious day-hike into the high terrain will not intersect with many other people and looks nothing like the rest of Japan.

sections.heading: Getting there
paragraphs: Getting there: Sapporo (New Chitose Airport) is 1h40m from Tokyo by air. Domestic flights are cheap 窶・book at least three weeks in advance for ﾂ･8,000窶・5,000 return. Once you're there, a rental car is the right choice for Furano and Biei; trains exist but the timing is inconvenient for covering multiple areas in a day.

title: Koenji and Shimokitazawa: Tokyo for People Who've Already Done Harajuku
excerpt: Two stops on different lines. Two neighborhoods that run on thrift stores, live music, and a specific refusal to be improved.
heroImageAlt: Narrow street in Shimokitazawa lined with vintage clothing shops and cafes
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What these neighborhoods are
paragraphs: Harajuku is famous. Shimokitazawa is known. Koenji is where people who've lived in Tokyo for five years go when they want to be reminded what the city felt like before everything got expensive and curated. Both Koenji and Shimokitazawa deserve an afternoon each, and the fastest way to understand why is to walk from the station in either direction without a destination.

sections.heading: Koenji: the vintage ecosystem
paragraphs: Koenji, on the Chuo Line, is Tokyo's vintage district 窶・not vintage-as-aesthetic but vintage-as-ecosystem. The streets around the north and south exits of the station contain somewhere between 50 and 80 second-hand clothing shops, each specializing in something different: one does only 70s Western denim, another specializes in military surplus, a third has nothing but vintage sportswear priced by the kilo. The Awa Odori festival in August draws around 300,000 people to this neighborhood specifically, making it briefly the loudest and most populated neighborhood in all of Tokyo.

sections.heading: Koenji: bars and izakaya
paragraphs: The coffee shops and bars in Koenji are a separate argument for visiting. Pure, a jazz bar in continuous operation since the 1980s, plays vinyl records on equipment that hasn't changed since opening. There are izakaya here that predate the bubble economy and have no interest in attracting anyone who found them on an app. The food is good and cheap: a tonkatsu set lunch is ﾂ･950, a yakitori skewer is ﾂ･120.

sections.heading: Shimokitazawa: live music
paragraphs: Shimokitazawa, accessible via the Odakyu Line from Shinjuku or Keio Inokashira from Shibuya, has a different character. It's the live music neighborhood: more than 30 small music venues (livehouse) are within a 10-minute walk of the station, ranging from 50-person basement rooms to 500-capacity halls. Any night of the week, most of them have shows. Tickets are typically ﾂ･2,000窶・,000 at the door.

sections.heading: The Sunday market
paragraphs: The Sunday market at Shimokitazawa draws vintage sellers, plant vendors, record collectors, and food stalls to the streets outside the station. It's a neighborhood event rather than a tourist attraction, and has the energy of something that's been happening every week for years and has no interest in being photographed for social media. Show up around 11am with no plan and spend two hours. That's the correct approach to Shimokitazawa.

title: How to Actually Eat Wagyu Beef in Japan
excerpt: Wagyu is expensive. This is not a misunderstanding. The question is what you're getting for the expense, and whether a ﾂ･5,000 lunch version is the same as a ﾂ･30,000 dinner.
heroImageAlt: Thinly sliced wagyu beef with visible marbling on a wooden board
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What wagyu actually is
paragraphs: Wagyu refers to four specific Japanese beef cattle breeds 窶・Black, Brown, Shorthorn, and Polled 窶・of which Japanese Black (Kuroge Wagashu) is the one you'll encounter at restaurants. The defining characteristic is intramuscular fat (shimofuri), distributed through the muscle in a marbling pattern that creates the butter-soft texture wagyu is known for. The marbling is graded A1 to A5 by the Japan Meat Grading Association; A5 is the maximum.

sections.heading: Kobe, Matsusaka, and Omi
paragraphs: The three regions you'll encounter most often. Kobe beef (from Tajima-strain cattle in Hyogo Prefecture): the most famous internationally, and genuinely excellent, though supply is far smaller than the global market for 'Kobe beef' implies 窶・most 'Kobe beef' outside Japan is not from Kobe. Matsusaka beef from Mie Prefecture is considered by many Japanese food professionals to be superior in fat quality; it's less famous internationally because nobody started a marketing campaign for it in the 1990s. Omi beef from Shiga, one of the oldest beef brands in Japan, is the option for people who've read enough about wagyu to want the historical footnote.

sections.heading: Formats and prices
paragraphs: Formats and pricing. A5 wagyu as yakiniku (grilled at the table over charcoal) is the most common restaurant format 窶・ﾂ･3,000窶・,000 for a plate of premium cuts, best shared. A5 wagyu sukiyaki (simmered in a sweet soy broth with tofu and vegetables) is the winter version and is slightly more forgiving for first-timers because the broth moderates the richness. Wagyu as a steak, Western-style, is available in department store restaurants and high-end steakhouses 窶・expect ﾂ･15,000窶・0,000 per person for the premium experience.

sections.heading: The accessible option
paragraphs: The practical option: department store basement food halls (depachika) in Tokyo, Osaka, and Kyoto sell wagyu by the slice for home cooking and often have prepared wagyu bentos and small yakiniku sets. A high-quality wagyu beef bowl (gyudon) at a specialist restaurant like Yoshida Gyu in Tokyo can be had for ﾂ･1,500窶・,000 and gives a genuine sense of what A4窶鄭5 wagyu tastes like without the full restaurant-dinner commitment.

sections.heading: The one thing worth knowing
paragraphs: The one thing worth knowing: wagyu at A5 is rich. A small amount satisfies in a way that lean beef doesn't. At a proper yakiniku restaurant, a 100g portion is enough for one person. The instinct to order more than you can eat is strong and almost always incorrect. Order one plate, taste it properly, and then decide if you want another.

title: Japan's Vending Machines Are a Serious Subject
excerpt: There are 4 million vending machines in Japan. One for every 31 people. This is not an accident.
heroImageAlt: A row of colourful Japanese vending machines on a city street at night
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The density
paragraphs: Japan has more vending machines per capita than any country except possibly Singapore. They appear outside train stations, on mountain trails, inside office buildings, at the end of rural roads where nothing else exists for kilometers. The density is a logistics achievement: the machines are serviced on fixed schedules, calibrated to local demand, and stocked with whatever the neighborhood apparently wants at the hour it apparently wants it.

sections.heading: What is for sale
paragraphs: What's for sale: the most common machines stock canned and bottled drinks 窶・hot canned coffee, cold green tea, sports drinks, canned beer, occasionally alcohol. The hot/cold switching happens seasonally and sometimes twice daily 窶・a button will show HOT or COLD depending on which side of the internal temperature shelf the product is resting on. Buying a hot Boss Coffee from a vending machine in February is one of those simple pleasures that people consistently describe as an unexpectedly significant memory from Japan.

sections.heading: Specialty machines
paragraphs: The specialty machines: some train stations have instant ramen machines with a surprisingly wide variety. Umbrella machines appear outside train stations when rain is forecast and charge ﾂ･500窶・00 for a basic transparent umbrella. There are machines selling fresh bananas, hot pizza by the slice, live stag beetles in summer (this is real), and surgical masks in quantities ranging from one to fifty.

sections.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing: drinks at vending machines are ﾂ･130窶・00 for most items, approximately the same as a convenience store. The premium is zero or marginal. Alcohol (beer, chuhai) is available late-night where the machine is located outside a convenience store or near a station 窶・age verification is on the honor system at the machine level, though some have a button for adult confirmation.

sections.heading: IC card payment
paragraphs: IC card payment: most modern vending machines accept Suica or ICOCA. Tap the card reader, the drink drops, and the amount is deducted. It's faster than coins, which in Japan are otherwise still the default payment method for vending. The transition to contactless is quicker here than at cash registers, perhaps because the machine doesn't need to be retrained.

sections.heading: The cultural point
paragraphs: The cultural point: vending machines in Japan are clean, they work (machine failure is rare enough to be noteworthy), and they stock things people actually want. This seems basic until you've been somewhere where vending machines are a coin trap of expired merchandise and broken change returns. Japan's machines are infrastructure, and like most Japanese infrastructure, they are taken seriously.

title: Day Trips From Tokyo That Aren't Hakone or Nikko
excerpt: Hakone is excellent. Nikko is impressive. Both are in every guidebook and overrun on weekends. There are better options if you're willing to look slightly further.
heroImageAlt: Kurazukuri Street in Kawagoe with traditional clay-walled Edo-period storehouses
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: The problem with the usual recommendations
paragraphs: The standard day-trip recommendations from Tokyo are Hakone (hot springs, Fuji views), Nikko (elaborate Edo-period shrines in the mountains), and Kamakura (giant Buddha, seaside). All three are worth visiting. All three are also predictable, and in peak season they're handling tourist numbers that test their infrastructure. If you've already been to any of them, or if you're planning a second Tokyo visit and want to branch out, there are options that deliver comparable experiences with significantly smaller crowds.

sections.heading: Kawagoe
paragraphs: Kawagoe, 30 minutes from Ikebukuro on the Tobu Tojo Line (around ﾂ･470). Called Little Edo, it has a preserved merchant district (Kurazukuri Street) with Edo-period clay-walled warehouses that have become antique shops, sweet shops, and cafﾃｩs. The bell tower that rings every six hours has been doing so since 1624. The Kashiya Yokocho (Candy Lane) alley sells old-fashioned Japanese sweets from small family shops. Significantly fewer international visitors than Kamakura; similar historical atmosphere.

sections.heading: Chichibu
paragraphs: Chichibu, 90 minutes from Ikebukuro on the Seibu Chichibu Line.
