A mountain town with a famous Shinto shrine (Chichibu Shrine), a series of 34 Buddhist pilgrimage temples that serious walkers circuit over multiple days, and the only natural karst formation in the Kanto region at Ootaki. In December, the Chichibu Night Festival (December 2窶・) is one of Japan's largest float festivals and draws 200,000 people 窶・still far more manageable than any equivalent Tokyo-area event.

sections.heading: Izu Peninsula
paragraphs: Izu Peninsula, 90 minutes from Tokyo by Shinkansen to Atami or Mishima. A volcanic peninsula with hot springs, dramatic coastline, and fewer tourists per kilometer than anywhere in the Hakone area. Shuzenji in the central valley is a small onsen town with a classic feel 窶・a bamboo grove along the river, wooden onsen hotels, a 9th-century Buddhist temple. Enough for a full day without a crowd problem.

sections.heading: Mashiko
paragraphs: Mashiko, two hours from Ueno by train (JR Utsunomiya plus bus). A ceramics town that became famous when the potter Shoji Hamada established a studio there in the 1920s and invited other ceramicists. There are now around 300 kilns in and around the town. The spring and autumn pottery fairs are among the best craft markets in Japan. The Hamada Shoji Memorial Mashiko Sankokan museum is on the original estate and worth two hours. The journey requires planning but the experience is unique.

title: Omiyage: The Art of Bringing Back the Right Thing
excerpt: Returning from anywhere in Japan without gifts for the people you left behind is not a neutral act. Omiyage culture runs deep, and buying well requires knowing the rules.
heroImageAlt: Beautifully packaged Japanese confectionery boxes displayed at a station gift shop
author: TABI Editorial

sections.heading: What omiyage is
paragraphs: Omiyage 窶・travel souvenirs intended for others 窶・is a structured practice in Japan, not an afterthought. When Japanese people return from a trip, they bring back regional food gifts for coworkers, family, and neighbors. The obligation is real enough that Japanese airports, train stations, and tourist areas are built around it: the gift shops at major stations are not concessions but primary retail destinations designed for the specific purpose of providing appropriately packaged regional food at prices calibrated for group gifting.

sections.heading: What makes good omiyage
paragraphs: What constitutes good omiyage. The best gifts are things that are genuinely regional 窶・a sweet or confection from a specific prefecture that can't be bought in Tokyo or wherever you're returning to. Kyoto's Yatsuhashi (cinnamon-flavored triangular confections, raw or baked, in a range of fillings), Hiroshima's Momiji Manju (maple-leaf-shaped cakes with red bean or cream filling), Sapporo's Royce chocolate 窶・these are the canonical examples, and they work because they represent a place specifically. Generic Japan gifts (things with sakura or Mt. Fuji printed on them) are less appreciated.

sections.heading: Format matters
paragraphs: Format matters. Omiyage is given in individually portioned packages, usually 6, 10, or 15 pieces, because it will be shared across a group. A single item that can't be divided is not omiyage. The packaging is typically beautiful and part of the gift 窶・Japanese confectionery packaging has been developed over centuries into a minor art form.

sections.heading: What to bring home
paragraphs: For the visitor bringing things home to other countries: apply the same logic. Regional Japanese sweets in their original packaging travel well, communicate where you've been specifically, and arrive looking intentional. Matcha Kit Kats and regional chip flavors are the casual version. For something more considered: Hikari Kinako (roasted soybean flour snacks from Kyoto), wagashi (traditional confections) from an established Kyoto confectioner like Toraya, or a small tin of gyokuro tea from Uji.

sections.heading: Where to buy
paragraphs: Where to buy: the station gift shops (ekimachibu) in major Shinkansen stations have the best regional selection for your exact route. Kyoto Station has an excellent floor of Kyoto-specific gifts. Tokyo Station's basement has confections from across Japan, which is useful if you forgot to buy in-region but want regional gifts. Department stores have the most curated selection at the highest price; convenience store gift sections exist but should be a last resort.

title: The Hidden Shrines of Kyoto Locals Still Keep Secret
summary: Beyond Fushimi Inari and Kinkaku-ji lies another Kyoto 窶・quiet, moss-covered, and entirely yours. We asked local monks and longtime residents where they actually go when the crowds become too much. These are the shrines that rarely appear in guidebooks.
heroImageAlt: A quiet moss-covered shrine path in Kyoto lined with stone lanterns

title: How to Spend 10 Days in Japan Without a Single Tourist Trap
summary: Most first-time visitors follow the same Golden Route 窶・Tokyo, Kyoto, Osaka 窶・and come home feeling like they saw a theme park. This itinerary takes a different approach: slower, deeper, and built around the neighborhoods that locals actually use.

title: The Perfect Route up Mt. Fuji: What Nobody Tells You
summary: Climbing Fuji is one of those experiences that sounds straightforward until you're doing it at 3am in a headlamp queue. The mountain rewards preparation and punishes assumptions 窶・here's what we learned from five ascents across different seasons.

title: Osaka in 3 Days: The Eating and Wandering Itinerary
summary: Osaka has a reputation as Japan's kitchen, but that barely scratches the surface. This city rewards those who eat their way through it without a plan 窶・we've structured the chaos into three days that still feel spontaneous.

title: Yakushima: Japan's Ancient Forest That Few Tourists Find
summary: A UNESCO-listed island covered in cedar trees that are older than many civilizations, Yakushima sits off the southern tip of Kyushu and sees a fraction of the visitors it deserves. The hike through Shiratani Unsuikyo inspired Princess Mononoke 窶・and it still feels that way.

title: How to Read a Japanese Menu Without Speaking Japanese
summary: Most Japanese restaurants don't have English menus, and that's not a problem once you know what to look for. A handful of kanji, some pattern recognition, and the willingness to point gets you through almost any restaurant in the country.

title: What Wabi-Sabi Really Means 窶・And Why the World Needs It Now
summary: The Japanese aesthetic of imperfection and transience offers something Western design philosophy rarely does: permission to be incomplete. Wabi-sabi isn't a style 窶・it's a way of perceiving, and learning to see it changes how you look at almost everything.

title: Kintsugi: The Art of Repairing Broken Things with Gold
summary: A 500-year-old technique for mending ceramics with lacquer mixed with gold, silver, or platinum has become a global metaphor for resilience. We visited a master craftsman in Kyoto to understand why kintsugi is about far more than broken bowls.

title: Hanami 2026: Where to See Cherry Blossoms Like a Local
summary: Skip the tourist crowds at Maruyama Park and Ueno. The best hanami spots are the ones you stumble into 窶・a neighborhood embankment, a school gate, a canal nobody bothered to list. We've found them, so you don't have to.

title: The Best Japanese Kitchen Knives to Bring Home
summary: Japanese knives are the single best souvenir you can carry out of Japan 窶・functional, beautiful, and something you'll use every day for decades. This guide covers every price point from a ﾂ･4,000 entry knife to the hand-forged blade worth saving up for.

title: Buying Matcha: Ceremonial vs. Culinary Grade Explained
summary: The matcha market is flooded with products that taste nothing like what you drink in a tea ceremony 窶・and the price tag is not a reliable guide. Here's how to read labels, what to taste for, and where to buy the real thing.

title: 10 Japanese Drugstore Products Worth Every Yen
summary: Japanese pharmacies are overwhelming in the best way 窶・walls of skincare, hair treatments, and stomach remedies at prices that make no sense until you try them. These are the ten products that actually earn their place in your luggage.

title: Where to Buy Authentic Yukata in Tokyo Without the Markup
summary: The tourist-district yukata sold near Asakusa are fine, but the ones sold at department store basements and specialist shops are extraordinary 窶・and often cheaper. This guide takes you past the obvious options to the places worth visiting.
